EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A sharp cold front lied across far southern Minnesota during the afternoon of Tuesday, June 2nd. Temperatures ahead of this front rose into the mid to upper 90s with 70s and 80s behind it. In addition, dew points dropped significantly behind the front across central Minnesota. ||During the late morning, and early afternoon, elevated convection developed across central Minnesota and moved eastward but continued to develop on the southwest flank. Numerous hail stones were reported, but most were sub-severe. A few stronger updrafts contributed to a few hail stones as large as golf ball size near Alexandria. As the storms moved farther to the southeast, reports of large hail was observed near Elk River. ||By the mid-late afternoon, severe thunderstorms developed along a cold front across far southern Minnesota. Initially, most of the reports were very large hail, south of the Minnesota River, from around Lake Crystal, Garden City, Vernon Center and Good Thunder. There was a measured hail stone of 2.5 inches in diameter near Garden City. There was another measured report of 2.0 inches near Waldorf.  ||All of the significant wind damage reports were from the areas around New Richland, Waldorf, Hollandale, Myrtle, Albert Lea, Lewisville, and other areas of south central Minnesota due to severe thunderstorm outflow winds.  The storms were |highly visible from miles away and dozens of people took pictures and video.

A strong mid-level trough in split flow moved out of the southwestern U.S. on the morning of April 12, and phased with a strong northern stream trough across the northern Plains by April 13. Intense low pressure developed across the central Plains and moved into the Great Lakes, with a strong frontogenetic band of precipitation from Nebraska to Minnesota. Convective instability produced some spotty thunder along with the rain changing to snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 2.0 inches 8 miles southeast of Hendricks. Winds gusted at times to 30 to 39 mph near Lake Benton between 0100 and 1000CST, with visibility frequently as low as one-half mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong mid-level trough in split flow moved out of the southwestern U.S. on the morning of April 12, and phased with a strong northern stream trough across the northern Plains by April 13. Intense low pressure developed across the central Plains and moved into the Great Lakes, with a strong frontogenetic band of precipitation from Nebraska to Minnesota. Convective instability produced some spotty thunder along with the rain changing to snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 6.0 inches at Tracy and 4.5 inches at Marshall. Winds at Marshall and Tracy gusted frequently from 25 to 35 mph, with visibilities less than a mile, and briefly as low as one-quarter to one-half mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitation continued well-below normal across southwest Minnesota, varying from 10 to 25 percent of normal.  Temperatures were mainly near to slightly below normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate (D1) drought evolved to severe (D2) drought conditions early in the month. Rainfall of less than 25 percent of normal along with near normal temperatures contributed to the worsening short-term agricultural drought. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level wave dropped southeast from the Red River Valley through southwest Minnesota by midday, igniting convection along a southeastward pushing boundary.  With unidirectional deep-layer shear around 30 knots and MLCAPE approaching 1500 J/kg, a few supercells developed with very large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to golf ball size pummeled crops in northeast Nobles county. Damage can be seen from satellite data. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm cell strengthened over central Otter Tail County and produced a funnel cloud.
A thunderstorm cell over southeast Otter Tail County produced a brief funnel cloud.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud tracked across the northeast corner of Parkers Prairie Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the very early morning hours of Friday, August 14th, sporadic slow moving severe thunderstorms formed across portions of west central Minnesota. These resulted in reports of large hail, strong wind gusts, and flash flooding. Prior to sunrise and through Friday morning, additional storms developed back westward into southeast North Dakota, and they continued to fire across west central Minnesota. These storms continued to produce large hail, gusty winds, and flash flooding. By mid-afternoon, an area of surface low pressure tracked through west central Minnesota, dragging a cold front behind it. Five tornadoes occurred during this period across Grant County, Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were blown down near Flat Lake.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado was the westernmost of 5 tornadoes that formed in Grant County. It formed and dissipated fairly quickly, likely tracking for less than a half mile. Slight marking was noted in a soybean field by video and a damage survey team. Peak winds were estimated at 65 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the very early morning hours of Friday, August 14th, sporadic slow moving severe thunderstorms formed across portions of west central Minnesota. These resulted in reports of large hail, strong wind gusts, and flash flooding. Prior to sunrise and through Friday morning, additional storms developed back westward into southeast North Dakota, and they continued to fire across west central Minnesota. These storms continued to produce large hail, gusty winds, and flash flooding. By mid-afternoon, an area of surface low pressure tracked through west central Minnesota, dragging a cold front behind it. Five tornadoes occurred during this period across Grant County, Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado was the fourth of five tornadoes in Grant County. The tornado crossed U. S. Highway 59 about two miles south of Elbow Lake, the south end of Pomme de Terre Lake, and the Tipsinah Mounds Golf Course. It snapped and uprooted numerous trees, several power poles, and ravaged corn fields. It tore down a small tower, roofing, and roof sections from homes and a golf course clubhouse. Peak winds were estimated at 115 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the very early morning hours of Friday, August 14th, sporadic slow moving severe thunderstorms formed across portions of west central Minnesota. These resulted in reports of large hail, strong wind gusts, and flash flooding. Prior to sunrise and through Friday morning, additional storms developed back westward into southeast North Dakota, and they continued to fire across west central Minnesota. These storms continued to produce large hail, gusty winds, and flash flooding. By mid-afternoon, an area of surface low pressure tracked through west central Minnesota, dragging a cold front behind it. Five tornadoes occurred during this period across Grant County, Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado was the fifth and southernmost of five tornadoes that occurred in Grant County. This tornado had a persistent wall cloud for several minutes prior to tornado development. The tornado quickly became wrapped in heavy rain. It snapped or uprooted several trees, and tore roofing off of a home and a pole shed before passing over highway 55, as confirmed by video and a storm damage survey. Peak winds were estimated at 95 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across eastern North Dakota and the northwest quarter of Minnesota on Sunday, August 9th. Abundant moisture remained in place, with dew points in the upper 60s to lower 70s. Thunderstorms formed across the area, producing large hail, damaging wind gusts, and tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large trees were blown down in the Big Bog State Park. The DNR was called in to help cut trees to assist trapped campers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Just after midnight, or in the early overnight hours of August 19th, a few showers and thunderstorms formed over western Marshall County (MN). By around 4 am CDT, one strong slow moving thunderstorm was located just northeast of Thief River Falls. Shortly thereafter, additional storms formed back to the west, into the Grand Forks (ND) area. These storms continued to progress slowly southeast through sunrise, producing multiple reports of large hail.
A cluster of thunderstorms tracked southeast out of southern Manitoba, through a good portion of Kittson County. These storms produced large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind gust was measured by a MNDOT RWIS mesonet sensor.

Abundant moisture remained in place, with dew points in the upper 60s to lower 70s. Thunderstorms formed across the area, producing large hail, damaging wind gusts, and tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large branches were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late afternoon and evening of Friday, August 7th, a warm front lifted through the northwest quarter of Minnesota. Thunderstorms fired up, and produced large hail, damaging wind gusts, and a few tornadoes.

During the very early morning hours of Friday, August 14th, sporadic slow moving severe thunderstorms formed across portions of west central Minnesota. These resulted in reports of large hail, strong wind gusts, and flash flooding. Prior to sunrise and through Friday morning, additional storms developed back westward into southeast North Dakota, and they continued to fire across west central Minnesota. These storms continued to produce large hail, gusty winds, and flash flooding. By mid-afternoon, an area of surface low pressure tracked through west central Minnesota, dragging a cold front behind it. Five tornadoes occurred during this period across Grant County, Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down at a golf course.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong, early season storm system passed across the Upper Midwest on December 23rd. This system produced blizzard to near blizzard conditions across portions of southeast Minnesota during the late afternoon and evening of the 23rd. One to three inches of new snowfall was whipped around by northwest winds gusting as high as 40 to 50 mph. This produced a 3 to 4 hour period when blowing snow reduced the visibility to a mile or less across Dodge, Olmsted and Mower Counties. The highest recorded wind gust was 50 mph by the automated weather observing equipment at the Rochester (Olmsted County) airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two to three inches of new snow were whipped around by wind gusts of 40 to 45 mph creating a period of blizzard to near blizzard conditions during the late afternoon and evening of December 23rd. The visibility was reduced to a mile or less by blowing snow between 5 and 930 p.m. The highest recorded wind gust by the automated weather observing equipment at the Dodge Center airport was 44 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong mid-level trough in split flow moved out of the southwestern U.S. on the morning of April 12, and phased with a strong northern stream trough across the northern Plains by April 13. Intense low pressure developed across the central Plains and moved into the Great Lakes, with a strong frontogenetic band of precipitation from Nebraska to Minnesota. Convective instability produced some spotty thunder along with the rain changing to snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 4.0 inches at Luverne. Winds near Beaver Creek gusted frequently from 30 to 40 mph, and as high as 45 mph between 0300 and 1400CST, with visibilities less than a mile, and frequently as low as one-quarter to one-half mile.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With cold air entrenched north of a warm front across southern Nebraska and Iowa, lift associated with a strong upper-level jet focused atop the cold dome resulted in widespread light freezing rain and drizzle, and some areas of snow deeper into the cold air.  The freezing rain developed during the late night and early morning hours, and generally ended by later morning or early afternoon. Ice accumulations varied from a few hundredths to almost a quarter inch, while accumulated snow after icing was less than an inch through the Highway 14 corridor in southwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light freezing drizzle started prior to sunrise and continued into early afternoon. Ice accumulation on elevated surfaces reached upward of one tenth of an inch, with lesser ice also accumulating on roads and bridges which produced dangerous conditions for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall across southwest Minnesota at the end of March resulted in rises on the West Fork of the Des Moines River, but only a brief climb into flood stage occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall over the last few days of March which totalled 1.15 inches at Windom and 8 miles north of Lakefield brought the West Fork of the Des Moines River (gage site WDOM5) to a crest of 0.16 feet above flood stage on April 3. Minor lowland flooding occurred along the east bank of the river near Windom.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface low pressure moved into western South Dakota on the afternoon of Friday, January 17th. To the east, or over portions of northwest and west central Minnesota, southeast winds became quite gusty. Many locations saw wind gusts between 30 and 40 mph. Along with some snow on the ground and some falling snow, low visibilities developed. Temperatures actually remained on the mild side, generally in the teens to low 20s. The low and a stationary boundary moved into western Minnesota Friday night. This brought a fairly short-lived period of lower winds to the area. As the front continued to push east, gusty northwest winds moved in. These northwest winds also brought gusts of 30 to 40 mph and an additional period of low visibilities. Colder temperatures also pushed into the area with the northwest winds. By late in the afternoon of Saturday, January 18th, temperatures had fallen to the single digits above and below zero. Most of the northwest quarter of Minnesota picked up 4 to 8 inches of snow, although a few locations saw up to 11 inches. Interstate 94 was closed from Fargo to Alexandria.

An area of low pressure developed over eastern Colorado on the 17th and moved northeast and deepened that evening, reaching Minnesota during the early morning hours of the 18th. Snow spread across the area ahead of the low and persisted into the 18th before the low moved into Michigan later in the day. Widespread snowfall totals of 6 to 10 inches were seen across the region with higher amounts in excess of a foot along the North Shore of Lake Superior where lake and terrain enhancement combined to produce heavier snowfall. Winds gusted to 30 to 40 mph on the 18th leading to areas of blowing and drifting snow and reduced visibility.

To the east, or over portions of northwest and west central Minnesota, southeast winds became quite gusty. Many locations saw wind gusts between 30 and 40 mph. Along with some snow on the ground and some falling snow, low visibilities developed. Temperatures actually remained on the mild side, generally in the teens to low 20s. The low and a stationary boundary moved into western Minnesota Friday night. This brought a fairly short-lived period of lower winds to the area. As the front continued to push east, gusty northwest winds moved in. These northwest winds also brought gusts of 30 to 40 mph and an additional period of low visibilities. Colder temperatures also pushed into the area with the northwest winds. By late in the afternoon of Saturday, January 18th, temperatures had fallen to the single digits above and below zero. Most of the northwest quarter of Minnesota picked up 4 to 8 inches of snow, although a few locations saw up to 11 inches. Interstate 94 was closed from Fargo to Alexandria.

An area of low pressure developed over eastern Colorado on the 17th and moved northeast and deepened that evening, reaching Minnesota during the early morning hours of the 18th. Snow spread across the area ahead of the low and persisted into the 18th before the low moved into Michigan later in the day. Widespread snowfall totals of 6 to 10 inches were seen across the region with higher amounts in excess of a foot along the North Shore of Lake Superior where lake and terrain enhancement combined to produce heavier snowfall. Winds gusted to 30 to 40 mph on the 18th leading to areas of blowing and drifting snow and reduced visibility.

A large storm system, which produced heavy snow during the onset, switched over to freezing drizzle, then back to light snow before transitioning to blowing snow and blizzard conditions. The storm started Friday morning, January 17th with a surge of initial deep moisture advecting northward across the Upper Midwest. A dry layer at mid-levels kept the snowfall across southwest, and south central Minnesota before this layer saturated by noon. There was a 3 to 5 hour period of heavy snowfall that produced snowfall rates of 1 inch per hour. A few areas in southwest Minnesota had already received 4 to 6 inches of snow before noon. This area of snow moved northeast and caused heavy snow bands across east central and central Minnesota, as well as west central Wisconsin by the afternoon. As quickly as the snow started, it ended by the late afternoon, and early evening as a dry slot at mid-levels cutoff any ice crystal producing clouds. This dry slot, and the accompany freezing drizzle once the snow ended, caused a layer of ice to form on the snow that fell earlier.||By sunrise, ice crystal producing clouds spread across the Upper Midwest as the main upper storm moved across the area. Periods of light snow fell across the region, but most of the accumulating snowfall had ended. The cold front moved across Minnesota, and into western Wisconsin by noon. Temperatures fell 10 to 20 degrees behind this front along with strong gusty winds developing. These winds created significant blowing and drifting snow in western Wisconsin, especially in open country. The winds slowly abated during the evening, but blowing snow continued.||A strong arctic cold front moved across Minnesota from before sunrise in the far west, to eastern Minnesota by late morning. The front was accompany by light to moderate snow which produced whiteout conditions for an hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several wind sensors, web cams, and trained observers, indicated that blizzard conditions started with the arrival of the cold frontal passage. By mid-morning, wind speeds increased to 25 to 35 mph, with gusts up to 45 mph. Whiteout conditions were observed across the county through the late afternoon. There was also numerous accidents around the county from the Minnesota Department of Transportation website.

The storm started Friday morning, January 17th with a surge of initial deep moisture advecting northward across the Upper Midwest. A dry layer at mid-levels kept the snowfall across southwest, and south central Minnesota before this layer saturated by noon. There was a 3 to 5 hour period of heavy snowfall that produced snowfall rates of 1 inch per hour. A few areas in southwest Minnesota had already received 4 to 6 inches of snow before noon. This area of snow moved northeast and caused heavy snow bands across east central and central Minnesota, as well as west central Wisconsin by the afternoon. As quickly as the snow started, it ended by the late afternoon, and early evening as a dry slot at mid-levels cutoff any ice crystal producing clouds. This dry slot, and the accompany freezing drizzle once the snow ended, caused a layer of ice to form on the snow that fell earlier.||By sunrise, ice crystal producing clouds spread across the Upper Midwest as the main upper storm moved across the area. Periods of light snow fell across the region, but most of the accumulating snowfall had ended. The cold front moved across Minnesota, and into western Wisconsin by noon. Temperatures fell 10 to 20 degrees behind this front along with strong gusty winds developing. These winds created significant blowing and drifting snow in western Wisconsin, especially in open country. The winds slowly abated during the evening, but blowing snow continued.||A strong arctic cold front moved across Minnesota from before sunrise in the far west, to eastern Minnesota by late morning. The front was accompany by light to moderate snow which produced whiteout conditions for an hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several wind sensors, web cams, and trained observers, indicated that blizzard conditions started with the arrival of the cold frontal passage. By mid-morning, wind speeds increased to 30 to 40 mph, with gusts up to 50 mph. Whiteout conditions were observed across the county through the late afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface low pressure moved into western South Dakota on the afternoon of Friday, January 17th. To the east, or over the eastern Dakotas and western Minnesota, southeast winds became quite gusty. Many locations saw wind gusts between 40 and 50 mph. Along with some snow on the ground and some falling snow, whiteout conditions developed. Temperatures actually remained on the mild side, generally in the teens to low 20s. As the low moved into western Minnesota Friday night, a stationary front also moved into the Red River Valley. This brought a fairly short-lived period of lower winds to the area. As the front continued to push east, gusty northwest winds moved in. These northwest winds also brought gusts of 40 to 50 mph and an additional period of whiteout conditions. Colder temperatures also pushed into the area with the northwest winds. By late in the afternoon of Saturday, January 18th, temperatures had fallen to the single digits above and below zero. Most of the Red River Valley region of the northwest quarter of Minnesota picked up 4 to 8 inches of snow, although a few locations saw up to 11 inches. Interstate 94 was closed from Fargo to Alexandria.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large storm system, which produced heavy snow during the onset, switched over to freezing drizzle, then back to light snow before transitioning to blowing snow and blizzard conditions. The storm started Friday morning, January 17th with a surge of initial deep moisture advecting northward across the Upper Midwest. A dry layer at mid-levels kept the snowfall across southwest, and south central Minnesota before this layer saturated by noon. There was a 3 to 5 hour period of heavy snowfall that produced snowfall rates of 1 inch per hour. A few areas in southwest Minnesota had already received 4 to 6 inches of snow before noon. This area of snow moved northeast and caused heavy snow bands across east central and central Minnesota, as well as west central Wisconsin by the afternoon. As quickly as the snow started, it ended by the late afternoon, and early evening as a dry slot at mid-levels cutoff any ice crystal producing clouds. Periods of light snow fell across the region, but most of the accumulating snowfall had ended. The cold front moved across Minnesota, and into western Wisconsin by noon. Temperatures fell 10 to 20 degrees behind this front along with strong gusty winds developing. These winds created significant blowing and drifting snow in western Wisconsin, especially in open country. The winds slowly abated during the evening, but blowing snow continued.||A strong arctic cold front moved across Minnesota from before sunrise in the far west, to eastern Minnesota by late morning. The front was accompany by light to moderate snow which produced whiteout conditions for an hour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level disturbance worked northeast from Colorado early morning January 12 into Iowa by late day. Broad lift initially within the warm advection zone became increasingly concentrated, with deeper lift in the dendritic growth layer by afternoon across northwest Iowa. Snowfall rates up to 1.5 to 2 inches per hour were noted across northwest Iowa and northeast Nebraska at the peak during the late afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate snowfall in the afternoon resulted in accumulation of 2.3 inches at Hills, resulting in hazardous road conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong surface low pressure system tracked from Wyoming across South Dakota on Friday, January 17th and Saturday, January 18th. Two periods of snowfall occurred with this system with the first snow event starting early to mid morning Friday across west central Minnesota and ending by late afternoon. Moderate to heavy snowfall with south winds from 30 to 40 mph gusting to 45 mph brought a period of blizzard conditions to west central Minnesota from the morning to the late afternoon on Friday. By Friday evening, a brief lull in the winds allowed for improvements to the visibility.||A second quick period of snowfall occurred in the early morning hours Saturday with an Arctic cold front coming in from the northwest.  Northwest winds of 30 to 40 mph gusting to over 50 mph behind the front brought a second round of blizzard conditions in the early morning hours Saturday. Ground blizzard conditions then continued through much of the day Saturday across west central Minnesota. Very cold air moved in with the northwest winds as well. Impacts included road closures or no travel advised along with vehicles in ditches. Some snowfall amounts include, 3 inches at Wheaton; and 4 inches at Artichoke Lake, Ortonville, and Browns Valley.

An area of low pressure developed over the Central Plains on the 28th and then moved northeast over southeastern Minnesota by the morning of the 29th. This spread snowfall across parts of the Northland and areas along and downwind of Lake Superior experienced lake and terrain enhancement of the snowfall. While areas immediately adjacent to Lake Superior along the North Shore and Twin Ports area did not receive heavy snowfall, areas inland above the terrain ridge saw between 6 and 13 inches of accumulation.

The afternoon of Tuesday, May 26th, a surface low was noted in southwest Iowa with a broad area of southeast flow along a surface boundary northward across southern Minnesota. Scattered thunderstorms started to develop in the warm sector by the mid afternoon across western Wisconsin and Iowa. These storms moved northward into an area of MLCAPEs of 1500-2000 J/kg with bulk shear values of around 30 knots. Radar indicated that numerous storms were rotating along the boundary as they moved northward. Most of the storms produced funnel clouds, but two storms caused an EF0 tornado east of Albert Lea, and another in the southeast Twin Cities suburbs of Rosemount. A line of storms that moved northward across southeast Minnesota produced wind damage in Kenyon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A deputy and several others observed a funnel cloud for several minutes. The funnel was also photographed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A canoe with three people aboard capsized on Tuscarora Lake within the Boundary Waters Canoe Area Wilderness at around 2PM the afternoon of the 20th. Two of the three occupants were able to reach an island and were rescued, but the third was unable to reach the island and was found deceased on the water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A canoe with three people aboard capsized on Tuscarora Lake within the Boundary Waters Canoe Area Wilderness at around 2PM the afternoon of the 20th. Winds at the time were from the south and gusting to 15 to 20 mph. Water temperatures were also extremely cold. All three occupants were wearing life jackets and two were able to reach a nearby island. The third did not and was found floating on the lake a short time later. The cause of death was listed as hypothermia and drowning.

Another tree was blown down across U.S. Highway 218 north of Lyle (Mower County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down across State Highway 16 east of Grand Meadow. The driver of a vehicle was unable to avoid the fallen tree and ran into it.

Another tree was blown down across U.S. Highway 218 north of Lyle (Mower County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down across U.S. Highway 218 north of Lyle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few strong thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and early evening hours in association with a cold front moving through the region.  One storm produced marginally severe hail near Embarrass before weakening as it slowly moved to the northeast.

An early April winter storm brought a mixture of freezing rain, sleet, and snow to eastern North Dakota and adjacent areas of the northwest quarter of Minnesota. Some areas received a quarter to half an inch of freezing rain and sleet, followed by over 4 inches of snow. A strip of 8 to 16 inches of snow fell from southwest Barnes County (ND), through the Grand Forks area, and up toward eastern Kittson County (MN). By April 2nd into the 3rd, northwest winds also gusted up to 45 mph at times. This resulted in some blowing and drifting snow in open areas.

By the early evening of Thursday, August 13th, an area of surface low pressure had set up near Mayville, North Dakota. A warm front extended out to the east-southeast from the low. South of the warm front, temperatures were in the mid to upper 80s, with dew points in the lower 70s. Thunderstorms broke out along and north of the warm front, producing large hail, damaging wind gusts, and two tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several 4 to 6 inch diameter branches were blown down.

Several thunderstorms, some severe, developed across northeast South Dakota during the late evening of Monday, April 6th. These storms moved across west-central Minnesota after midnight. There were several reports of hail (possibly a swath of hail) with a measured hail size of one inch over northwest Douglas County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few reports of large hail, measured up to one inch in diameter (Along the Grant/Douglas County line near Ashby) occurred over northwest Douglas County. The swath of hail extended to the northeast, close to Millersville, where lots of pea to dime size fell.

Late Friday night, and into Saturday, April 12th, a strong system started organizing across the northern and central Rockies, with moisture building in the central plains ahead of the system.  A cold front moved as far south as southeast Minnesota, and west central Wisconsin before stalling Saturday night. A surface low developed across Colorado Saturday, before intensifying and moving east-northeast across the plains, and into the Great Lakes by Sunday night. Most of central and southern Minnesota, as well as west central Wisconsin were in the cold side of this storm system. Only the far southeast part had enough warm air to cause some mixing of sleet, and rain before changing over to snow Sunday. This was noted with the next day visible satellite image which had a sharp cutoff of where no snow accumulated in the far southeast part of Minnesota. ||Due to the initial weak short waves ahead of the main storm system moving out into the plains Sunday morning, some light snow began in portions of southwest and south central Minnesota. Most of the snowfall prior to sunrise Sunday was light with light accumulations. Once stronger forcing develop Sunday morning, does the snowfall intensity increase. The initial band of heavier snowfall was noted in south central Minnesota where 3 to 6 inches fell before early Sunday afternoon. By the afternoon, a secondary band of heavier snowfall began in southwest Minnesota, and slowly moved eastward during the afternoon, and into the early evening across west central Wisconsin before weakening. This band of heavier snowfall was related to strong forcing in the 850-700 MB layer. Snowfall amounts of 6 inches or more occurred in this band by the mid afternoon before  it tapered off and moved east into Wisconsin. This is when the bulk of the snow accumulated but lighter snowfall still remained over south central and east central Minnesota before ending Sunday evening. ||The following are some of the higher snowfall totals:||8.7 inches around Owatonna.|7.5 inches near Lakeville. |7.3 inches near Prior Lake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Most of the county had between 4 to 6 inches of snow during this event with a report of 7 inches around Mankato.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southeast Minnesota started April with some river flooding along portions of the  Mississippi River. The flooding was the result of runoff from snow that melted in late March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from melted snow pushed the Mississippi River out of its banks in Winona. The river crested over a foot above the flood stage at 14.2 feet on April 4th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A significant late season storm system dropped heavy snow on portions of southeast Minnesota on Easter Sunday, April 12th. The driver of a car was killed when they collided with a tow truck parked on the shoulder of U.S. Highway 52 in Rochester. Snowfall totals ranged from 4 to 10 inches and the highest reported total was 10.8 inches in Elgin (Wabasha County). As the heavy snow fell, roads quickly became snow covered and the treacherous travel conditions continued into the evening as strong northwest winds created blowing and drifting snow. With 7.5 inches in Rochester (Olmsted County), this was second highest Easter snowfall on record.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that 7 to 9 inches of snow fell across Dodge County. The highest reported total was 9 inches in Mantorville. Strong northwest winds created blowing and drifting snow during the late afternoon and evening hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moved across southeast Minnesota during the late afternoon and evening of June 28th. These storms produced two small tornadoes across Winona County. The first occurred northeast of Lewiston with very little damage reported. The second tornado touched down east of Stockton and also produced very little damage. The storms also dropped large hail ranging from nickel up to golf ball sized. The golf ball sized hail fell west of Lyle (Mower County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball sized hail fell west of Lyle.

By the early evening of Thursday, August 13th, an area of surface low pressure had set up near Mayville, North Dakota. A warm front extended out to the east-southeast from the low. South of the warm front, temperatures were in the mid to upper 80s, with dew points in the lower 70s. Thunderstorms broke out along and north of the warm front, producing large hail, damaging wind gusts, and two tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime to quarter sized hail fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early April winter storm brought a mixture of freezing rain, sleet, and snow to eastern North Dakota and adjacent areas of the northwest quarter of Minnesota. Some areas received a quarter to half an inch of freezing rain and sleet, followed by over 4 inches of snow. A strip of 8 to 16 inches of snow fell from southwest Barnes County (ND), through the Grand Forks area, and up toward eastern Kittson County (MN). By April 2nd into the 3rd, northwest winds also gusted up to 45 mph at times. This resulted in some blowing and drifting snow in open areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The most significant winter storm during January started as a surge of warm advection, followed by a strong trough through the southern Rockies on Friday, January 17. A period of moderate to heavy snowfall swept across the area, accompanied by southerly winds gusting to 35 to 45 mph. Snowfall then tapered off to a period of light freezing rain and freezing drizzle into the evening hours, before changing back to snow. By late evening and early in the overnight, winds transitioned to northwest and increased to 35 to 45 mph, with gusts of 50 to 60 mph common as low pressure intensified across Iowa. Strong winds persisted most of Saturday as the area of low pressure moved slowly toward the western Great Lakes. Most areas received between 4 and 8 inches of snowfall as well as a glaze of ice, with just shy of a quarter inch of ice in the Sioux City area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of heavy snowfall with winds gusting to 30 to 40 mph moved through during the morning and early afternoon of January 17, before tapering to several hours of freezing drizzle.  Ice accumulations were generally a glaze to a tenth of an inch, before precipitation changed back to snow in the evening.  Winds then picked up from the northwest, with peak wind gusts during the morning of January 18 reaching 59 mph at Luverne and 48 mph near Beaver Creek. Combined with snowfall and previous snow, winds produced numerous hours with visibility of around one-quarter mile or less, with travel not recommended. Total snowfall reached 7.0 inches at Hills.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level disturbance worked northeast from Colorado early morning January 12 into Iowa by late day. Broad lift initially within the warm advection zone became increasingly concentrated, with deeper lift in the dendritic growth layer by afternoon across northwest Iowa. Snowfall rates up to 1.5 to 2 inches per hour were noted across northwest Iowa and northeast Nebraska at the peak during the late afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to briefly heavy snowfall in the afternoon resulted in accumulation of 4.0 inches at Worthington, resulting in hazardous road conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The most significant winter storm during January started as a surge of warm advection, followed by a strong trough through the southern Rockies on Friday, January 17. A period of moderate to heavy snowfall swept across the area, accompanied by southerly winds gusting to 35 to 45 mph. Snowfall then tapered off to a period of light freezing rain and freezing drizzle into the evening hours, before changing back to snow. By late evening and early in the overnight, winds transitioned to northwest and increased to 35 to 45 mph, with gusts of 50 to 60 mph common as low pressure intensified across Iowa. Strong winds persisted most of Saturday as the area of low pressure moved slowly toward the western Great Lakes. Most areas received between 4 and 8 inches of snowfall as well as a glaze of ice, with just shy of a quarter inch of ice in the Sioux City area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A period of heavy snowfall with winds gusting to 30 to 40 mph moved through during the morning and early afternoon of January 17, before tapering to several hours of freezing drizzle.  Ice accumulations were generally a glaze, before precipitation changed back to snow in the evening.  Winds then picked up from the northwest, with peak wind gusts around midday January 18 reaching 46 mph 5 miles south of Hendricks and 44 mph near Lake Benton. Combined with snowfall and previous snow, winds produced numerous hours with visibility of around one-quarter mile or less, with travel not recommended. Total snowfall reached 3.7 inches 8 miles southeast of Hendricks. At 1647CST January 18, a vehicle lost control on snow-covered U.S. Highway 75 just south of the intersection with Township Road 46 and rolled onto its roof, resulting in an (indirect) injury to the driver.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon of Tuesday, June 9th, a broken line of thunderstorms developed from Redwood Falls, northeast to Willmar, and Little Falls. These storms developed along a sharp boundary where winds shifted from northwest to southeast in a small area.||Although instability wasn't strong, there was enough wind shear in the lower layer of the atmosphere to cause the storms to rotate. Once these storms had adequate rotation, a few developed funnel clouds and one storm dropped a tornado north of Danube in an open field. No damage was reported but it was captured on video for a few minutes which lifted dust from the farm field.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm moved across the Upper Midwest on January 17th and 18th. The storm generally dropped 3 to 5 inches of snow across the area followed by a light coating of ice from freezing drizzle. Despite the snow becoming ice crusted, wind gusts of 35 to 45 mph broke up the ice and snow, producing blowing snow and blizzard conditions from the morning of the 18th through much of the afternoon. The visibility was reduced to around a quarter of a mile during the blizzard conditions with drifting snow impacting most roads. The highest recorded wind gust was 53 mph by the automated weather observing equipment at the Rochester airport (Olmsted County). Due to the hazardous conditions, Dodge County pulled their snowplows off the roads on the 18th and travel was not advised across the county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported that 3 to 4 inches of snow fell across Wabasha County. A period of freezing drizzle created a coating of ice on top of the snow. Some blowing and drifting snow occurred during the morning and afternoon of the 18th with wind gusts of 30 to 40 mph. The highest recorded wind gust was 38 mph by a mesonet station near Kellogg.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening of Thursday, January 30th, a band of snow developed across northeast South Dakota. There was a broad circulation over central Minnesota due to a slowly moving storm system centered near Alexandria. This circulation moved little, or drifted eastward during the night. On the southwest side of this circulation, a band of snow developed, and persisted eastward across portions of west central Minnesota. Radar indicated the persistent band moved across Yellow Medicine, and northern Lincoln, Lyon counties for several hours. By 3 am, this band moved out of the area, but persisted east, and northeast as the center of circulation moved southward toward St. Cloud. There was a very narrow band of 4 to 8 inches of snow that fell near Canby, eastward to north of Granite Falls. 4 inch snowfall amounts also occurred in portions of Renville County to the east, and another area of 2 to 4 inches from Litchfield, northeast to St. Cloud. There was one isolated 9 inch total northeast of Minneota, near the border of Yellow Medicine and Lyon Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trained spotters and snowfall observers, measured 4 to 8 inches of snow in a narrow band from Canby, east to Hanley Falls. An isolated measurement of 9 inches occurred near the border of Lyon and Yellow Medicine Counties, northeast of Minneota.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southeast Minnesota started April with some river flooding along portions of the  Mississippi River. The flooding was the result of runoff from snow that melted in late March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from melted snow pushed the Mississippi River out of its banks in Wabasha. The river crested over a foot above the flood stage at 13.3 feet on April 4th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure intensified moving from southeast Colorado on the morning of March 19, into the Great Lakes early on March 20. A strong band of mid-level frontogenesis progressed slowly eastward across the area during the afternoon and evening hours. The system was initially quite warm, resulting in a swath of moderate to heavy rainfall from northeast Nebraska into northwest Iowa. As the system strengthened on the 19th, much colder air was dragged into the frontal band, and precipitation turned quickly from rain to snow from northwest to southeast, and was accompanied by winds gusting 40 to 55 mph. Snowfall was generally from 1 to 5 inches near and south of Interstate 90, with heaviest bands near the Missouri River and again from the Sioux Falls area eastward along Interstate 90.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain changed quickly to snowfall late afternoon on March 19, with visibility one-quarter to one-half mile at times between 1600-1900CST. The intense snowfall rate made travel hazardous during the evening as winds increased to a peak of 50 mph near Beaver Creek and 40 mph at Luverne. Snowfall totaled 4.1 inches at Hills.

A strong band of mid-level frontogenesis progressed slowly eastward across the area during the afternoon and evening hours. The system was initially quite warm, resulting in a swath of moderate to heavy rainfall from northeast Nebraska into northwest Iowa. As the system strengthened on the 19th, much colder air was dragged into the frontal band, and precipitation turned quickly from rain to snow from northwest to southeast, and was accompanied by winds gusting 40 to 55 mph. Snowfall was generally from 1 to 5 inches near and south of Interstate 90, with heaviest bands near the Missouri River and again from the Sioux Falls area eastward along Interstate 90.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain changed quickly to snowfall by early afternoon on March 19, with visibility briefly around one-half mile at times between 1400-1700CST. The intense snowfall rate made travel hazardous during the late afternoon and early evening as winds increased to a peak of 43 mph at Marshall and 38 mph at Tracy. Snowfall totaled 1.0 inch at Marshall and Tracy.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The afternoon of Tuesday, May 26th, a surface low was noted in southwest Iowa with a broad area of southeast flow along a surface boundary northward across southern Minnesota. Scattered thunderstorms started to develop in the warm sector by the mid afternoon across western Wisconsin and Iowa. These storms moved northward into an area of MLCAPEs of 1500-2000 J/kg with bulk shear values of around 30 knots. Radar indicated that numerous storms were rotating along the boundary as they moved northward. Most of the storms produced funnel clouds, but two storms caused an EF0 tornado east of Albert Lea, and another in the southeast Twin Cities suburbs of Rosemount. A line of storms that moved northward across southeast Minnesota produced wind damage in Kenyon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A local emergency manager Meteorologist observed a brief touchdown northeast of Rosemount. Damage was confined to a few trees blown down as the EF0 tornado moved northeast and dissipated in an open field.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved from Manitoba into northwestern Ontario during the afternoon of June 1. A warm front pushed across the Northland during the morning hours and brought a surge of moisture ahead of a cold front. Storms developed ahead of the warm front during the morning hours and ahead of the cold front during the afternoon and evening hours. While most storms were sub-severe, a few produced quarter to ping pong ball-sized hail with one storm producing hail up to the size of tennis balls.

The morning of Wednesday, February 12th, an Arctic front moved rapidly southward across the Dakotas, and into Minnesota. Temperatures fell 10 to 20 degrees in less than a couple hours as this front moved through the area. Along with the falling temperatures, wind speeds increased to 25 to 35 mph, with gusts in the 40 to 50 mph range in west central Minnesota by mid-morning, and across southwest/south central Minnesota by afternoon. Light snow accompanied the front, but as winds increased, visibility dropped to less than one quarter of a mile. This was noted on several weather sensors, including the Minnesota Department of Transportation. Local webcams, and MNDOT mobile cameras also supported the blizzard conditions. The worst conditions occurred along the South Dakota, Minnesota border where the strongest winds were reported. Satellite imagery also noted the contrast of where blowing snow occurred, and where skies were clear. ||By the mid to late afternoon, temperatures fell well below zero in west-central Minnesota, with wind chills already exceeding -35F in Stevens County. These cold wind chills expanded south and east across Minnesota, and by the evening a broad area of wind chill values of -35 to -45 degrees developed. These wind chill values lasted through Thursday morning before temperatures rose, and wind speeds decreased.||Some of the lowest wind chill values during this time include:|-48F at Benson.|-46 at St. James.|-45 at Fairmont.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Weather sensors indicated that wind chill values dropped below -35F by early Thursday morning, February 13th. These wind chill values continued through Thursday morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface high pressure built into the central Dakotas by the morning of Thursday, February 13th. With the center of the high being west of eastern North Dakota and the northwest quarter of Minnesota, wind speeds remained steady in the 5 to 10 mph range. This, combined with temperatures from 20 below to 30 below zero, created wind chills of 40 below to 50 below zero.

After a strong cold front moved across the Upper Midwest, dangerously cold wind chills occurred across portions of southeast Minnesota from the late evening of February 13th into the morning of the 14th. Wind chills of 30 to 40 below were common with the coldest values of 44 below occurring near Canton (Fillmore County) and in Rochester (Olmsted County). Several schools across the region utilized a two late start on the 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill values of 30 to 40 below zero were common across Dodge County during the late evening of the 13th into the morning of the 14th. The coldest recorded wind chill was 41 below at Dodge Center. Some schools across the county used a two hour late start.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure developed across the northern Rockies on Saturday, February 8th. This area of low pressure moved and weakened across South Dakota, and over northern Iowa by Sunday morning. However, a 120 knot jet across the Plains, combined with strong forcing at mid-levels of the atmosphere, generated a band of moderate to heavy snowfall across southern Minnesota starting early Sunday morning. This band of snow quickly moved across the southern part of the state by noon, and exited west central Wisconsin by the early afternoon. Snowfall rates as high as 1-2 inches per hour occurred with the most intense bands of snow. Lightning was also detected near Owatonna during the height of the storm. There was even an elevated instability layer along the nose of the intensifying low level jet that set up Sunday morning.||By noon Sunday, there was a band of 6 inches or greater of snowfall that fell from near Watertown in east central South Dakota, eastward to Granite Falls, Olivia, Hutchinson, the southern part of the Twin Cities Metro area, and to Eau Claire in Wisconsin. Inside of this 6 inch band, there was a heavier swath of 8 to 12 inches that fell from Canby to Redwood Falls, Olivia, Gaylord, and New Prague in Minnesota. There were even a couple of heavier reports of 14 inches at Lucan and 16 inches in Lake Crystal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several observers that measure snowfall across southern McLeod County, reported 6 to 8 inches of snow before it tapered off late Sunday morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong southerly return flow, gusting up to 55 mph, produced considerable blowing and drifting of previously fallen snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted as high as 50 mph 4 miles east northeast of Jeffers, 46 mph at Storden and 40 mph at Windom, which produced occasional widespread visibility as low as one mile from blowing snow. Travel became hazardous due to the low visibility and icy roads developing from drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface high pressure built into the central Dakotas by the morning of Thursday, February 13th. With the center of the high being west of eastern North Dakota and the northwest quarter of Minnesota, wind speeds remained steady in the 5 to 10 mph range. This, combined with temperatures from 20 below to 30 below zero, created wind chills of 40 below to 50 below zero.

After a strong cold front moved across the Upper Midwest, dangerously cold wind chills occurred across portions of southeast Minnesota from the late evening of February 13th into the morning of the 14th. Wind chills of 30 to 40 below were common with the coldest values of 44 below occurring near Canton (Fillmore County) and in Rochester (Olmsted County). Several schools across the region utilized a two late start on the 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills of 30 to 40 below were common across Winona County during the morning of February 14th. The coldest recorded wind chill was 38 below by a mesonet station near Ridgeway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful Arctic cold front moved quickly across the region during the morning hours bringing 1 to 2 inches of snowfall, very strong north winds of 30 to 40 mph gusting up to 50 mph, along with falling wind chills. With the new snowfall along with the existing snow cover, widespread blizzard conditions occurred across west central Minnesota.  After the blizzard, wind chills quickly became extreme across the region.  Schools were closed and travel was significantly disrupted due to the blizzard and extreme wind chills. See the storm data entry on the extreme wind chills.

An area of low pressure developed across the northern Rockies on Saturday, February 8th. This area of low pressure moved and weakened across South Dakota, and over northern Iowa by Sunday morning. However, a 120 knot jet across the Plains, combined with strong forcing at mid-levels of the atmosphere, generated a band of moderate to heavy snowfall across southern Minnesota starting early Sunday morning. This band of snow quickly moved across the southern part of the state by noon, and exited west central Wisconsin by the early afternoon. Snowfall rates as high as 1-2 inches per hour occurred with the most intense bands of snow. Lightning was also detected near Owatonna during the height of the storm. There was even an elevated instability layer along the nose of the intensifying low level jet that set up Sunday morning.||By noon Sunday, there was a band of 6 inches or greater of snowfall that fell from near Watertown in east central South Dakota, eastward to Granite Falls, Olivia, Hutchinson, the southern part of the Twin Cities Metro area, and to Eau Claire in Wisconsin. Inside of this 6 inch band, there was a heavier swath of 8 to 12 inches that fell from Canby to Redwood Falls, Olivia, Gaylord, and New Prague in Minnesota. There were even a couple of heavier reports of 14 inches at Lucan and 16 inches in Lake Crystal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several observers that measure snowfall across Steele County, reported 8 to 10 inches of snow before it tapered off late Sunday morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The morning of Wednesday, February 12th, an Arctic front moved rapidly southward across the Dakotas, and into Minnesota. Temperatures fell 10 to 20 degrees in less than a couple hours as this front moved through the area. Along with the falling temperatures, wind speeds increased to 25 to 35 mph, with gusts in the 40 to 50 mph range in west central Minnesota by mid-morning, and across southwest/south central Minnesota by afternoon. Light snow accompanied the front, but as winds increased, visibility dropped to less than one quarter of a mile. This was noted on several weather sensors, including the Minnesota Department of Transportation. Local webcams, and MNDOT mobile cameras also supported the blizzard conditions. The worst conditions occurred along the South Dakota, Minnesota border where the strongest winds were reported. Satellite imagery also noted the contrast of where blowing snow occurred, and where skies were clear. ||By the mid to late afternoon, temperatures fell well below zero in west-central Minnesota, with wind chills already exceeding -35F in Stevens County. These cold wind chills expanded south and east across Minnesota, and by the evening a broad area of wind chill values of -35 to -45 degrees developed. These wind chill values lasted through Thursday morning before temperatures rose, and wind speeds decreased.||Some of the lowest wind chill values during this time include:|-48F at Benson.|-46 at St. James.|-45 at Fairmont.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several weather sensors, including the Minnesota Department of Transportation webcams, and local observations, indicated blizzard conditions by early afternoon across Redwood County. Winds were gusting up to 30 to 40 mph, with surface visibility less than one quarter of a mile at the Tracy and Redwood Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Fresh snowfall on the 12th of February followed by an influx of very cold air and building high pressure led to a very cold morning of the 13th. Enough of a gradient remained to create light winds overnight resulting in wind chills of 40 to 50 degrees below zero over much of northeastern Minnesota.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure developed across the northern Rockies on Saturday, February 8th. This area of low pressure moved and weakened across South Dakota, and over northern Iowa by Sunday morning. However, a 120 knot jet across the Plains, combined with strong forcing at mid-levels of the atmosphere, generated a band of moderate to heavy snowfall across southern Minnesota starting early Sunday morning. This band of snow quickly moved across the southern part of the state by noon, and exited west central Wisconsin by the early afternoon. Snowfall rates as high as 1-2 inches per hour occurred with the most intense bands of snow. Lightning was also detected near Owatonna during the height of the storm. There was even an elevated instability layer along the nose of the intensifying low level jet that set up Sunday morning.||By noon Sunday, there was a band of 6 inches or greater of snowfall that fell from near Watertown in east central South Dakota, eastward to Granite Falls, Olivia, Hutchinson, the southern part of the Twin Cities Metro area, and to Eau Claire in Wisconsin. Inside of this 6 inch band, there was a heavier swath of 8 to 12 inches that fell from Canby to Redwood Falls, Olivia, Gaylord, and New Prague in Minnesota. There were even a couple of heavier reports of 14 inches at Lucan and 16 inches in Lake Crystal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several observers that measure snowfall across southern Washington County, reported 6 to 8 inches of snow before it tapered off late Sunday morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One person was killed in a two vehicle accident on February 7th south of Blooming Prairie (Dodge County). The two vehicles collided with one catching fire after the collision, killing the driver. The accident report indicated the road was snow and ice covered at the time of the accident and automated weather observing equipment at the Austin airport (Mower County) and Dodge Center Airport (Dodge County) were both reporting light snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One person was killed in a two vehicle accident south of Blooming Prairie on the morning of February 7th. After the collision, one of the vehicles caught on fire. The roads were reported to be snow and ice covered at the time of the accident and the automated weather observing equipment at the Austin and Dodge Center airports were both reporting light snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two people were killed and two more were injured in a single vehicle accident on Interstate 90 near Dexter (Mower County) on February 13th. The driver of the vehicle lost control when encountering an icy stretch of road, went through the median before stopping in the opposing lanes of traffic. The two people killed where thrown from the vehicle. A period of light snow during the evening of February 12th followed by northwest winds of 15 to 25 mph created some blowing and drifting snow that led to the icy road conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two people were killed and two more were injured in a single vehicle accident on Interstate 90 near Dexter. Light snow fell during the evening of February 12th followed by northwest winds of 15 to 25 mph into the morning of the 13th. This created some blowing and drifting snow and caused icy conditions to develop on some roads. The driver of the vehicle lost control on an icy spot, went through the median before coming to a stop in the opposite lanes of traffic. The two people killed were thrown from the vehicle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A confluent trough digging into the Northern Plains established a tight mid-level frontal band which focused snowfall as it traversed the region. The band remained nearly stationary for 6 to 9 hours from east central South Dakota into southwest Minnesota. Instability led to further contraction of the frontal band with a very intense gradient from little snow to a half-foot or more.  Isolated thundersnow was reported across southwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall was heavy at times through early morning February 9. Snowfall accumulated to  8.5 inches 4 miles southwest of Ivanhoe.  Travel was not recommended during early to mid-morning February 9.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The morning of Wednesday, February 12th, an Arctic front moved rapidly southward across the Dakotas, and into Minnesota. Along with the falling temperatures, wind speeds increased to 25 to 35 mph, with gusts in the 40 to 50 mph range in west central Minnesota by mid-morning, and across southwest/south central Minnesota by afternoon. Light snow accompanied the front, but as winds increased, visibility dropped to less than one quarter of a mile. This was noted on several weather sensors, including the Minnesota Department of Transportation. Local webcams, and MNDOT mobile cameras also supported the blizzard conditions. The worst conditions occurred along the South Dakota, Minnesota border where the strongest winds were reported. Satellite imagery also noted the contrast of where blowing snow occurred, and where skies were clear. ||By the mid to late afternoon, temperatures fell well below zero in west-central Minnesota, with wind chills already exceeding -35F in Stevens County. These cold wind chills expanded south and east across Minnesota, and by the evening a broad area of wind chill values of -35 to -45 degrees developed. These wind chill values lasted through Thursday morning before temperatures rose, and wind speeds decreased.||Some of the lowest wind chill values during this time include:|-48F at Benson.|-46 at St. James.|-45 at Fairmont.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Weather sensors indicated that wind chill values dropped below -35F by mid afternoon of Wednesday, February 12th. These wind chill values continued until Thursday morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front moved through the Northern Plains during the morning of Wednesday, February 12th. Despite being accompanied by only an inch or so of snow, north winds gusted from 40 to 60 mph, causing blizzard conditions, especially within the Red River Valley. During the blizzard, Interstate 29 was closed from the Canadian border to the South Dakota border in North Dakota.

A winter storm moved rather quickly across the region on February 9th. This storm produced a period of heavy snow with rates of 1 to 2 inches an hour at times. Accumulations across southeast Minnesota were generally in the 6 to 10 inch range. The highest reported amounts were 10.8 inches near Stockton (Winona County) and 10.5 inches on the northeast side of Rochester (Olmsted County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: COOP and volunteer snow observers reported 6 to 10 inches of snow across Dodge County. The highest reported total was 10.3 inches in Kasson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure developed across the northern Rockies on Saturday, February 8th. This area of low pressure moved and weakened across South Dakota, and over northern Iowa by Sunday morning. However, a 120 knot jet across the Plains, combined with strong forcing at mid-levels of the atmosphere, generated a band of moderate to heavy snowfall across southern Minnesota starting early Sunday morning. This band of snow quickly moved across the southern part of the state by noon, and exited west central Wisconsin by the early afternoon. Snowfall rates as high as 1-2 inches per hour occurred with the most intense bands of snow. Lightning was also detected near Owatonna during the height of the storm. There was even an elevated instability layer along the nose of the intensifying low level jet that set up Sunday morning.||By noon Sunday, there was a band of 6 inches or greater of snowfall that fell from near Watertown in east central South Dakota, eastward to Granite Falls, Olivia, Hutchinson, the southern part of the Twin Cities Metro area, and to Eau Claire in Wisconsin. Inside of this 6 inch band, there was a heavier swath of 8 to 12 inches that fell from Canby to Redwood Falls, Olivia, Gaylord, and New Prague in Minnesota. There were even a couple of heavier reports of 14 inches at Lucan and 16 inches in Lake Crystal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several observers that measure snowfall across Lac Qui Parle County, reported locally 6 inches along the border of Yellow Medicine.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system that had a weak surface low near south central Minnesota, and a subtle disturbance, produced snowfall totals of 6 to 10 inches around New Ulm, and communities north and east for approximately 20 miles. Most areas received less than 4 inches, but even these amounts were not anticipated based on the weak storm, and short duration of this event. ||There were three small swaths of 3 to 5 inches that fell from this storm. The first fell from St. James, northward to New Ulm and Gaylord. The next band fell from St. James, eastward to Mapleton, Wells and Alberta Lea. The last band fell from near Buffalo, northeast to Elk River, Cambridge and Mora.||All of the precipitation occurred between 1230 PM and 9 PM, Monday, February 17th. Initially, only light snow was falling across southwest Minnesota before 2 PM. After 130 PM, a larger area of precipitation began to form across central, and southwest Minnesota. This area of snow moved eastward, but isolated heavier bands moved from southwest to the northeast.||Radar indicated an intense band of heavy snow around New Ulm for two hours. This led to snowfall totals around New Ulm around 10 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few weather observers measured between 5 and 8 inches across Sibley County. Snowfall rates as high as 1-1.5 inches per hour occurred between 2 PM and 5 PM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small area of light wintry mix developed within weak warm advection across southwest Minnesota and northwest Iowa. While amounts were light, it was enough to produce some slick road surfaces.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief period of light freezing rain with a few hundredths of an inch precipitation resulted in spotty slick roadways.  At 840CST, a vehicle just north of the Iowa-Minnesota state line on Highway 86 lost control on icy roadways, entered the ditch and rolled, resulting in (indirect) injuries to the driver and two passengers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A confluent trough digging into the Northern Plains established a tight mid-level frontal band which focused snowfall as it traversed the region. The band remained nearly stationary for 6 to 9 hours from east central South Dakota into southwest Minnesota. Instability led to further contraction of the frontal band with a very intense gradient from little snow to a half-foot or more.  Isolated thundersnow was reported across southwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall was moderate to heavy at times through early morning February 9. Snowfall accumulated to 6.3 inches at Windom.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to briefly heavy snowfall through early morning February 9 accumulated to 5.3 inches at Pipestone and 5.0 inches near Edgerton. Travel was not recommended during early to mid-morning on February 9.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The morning of Wednesday, February 12th, an Arctic front moved rapidly southward across the Dakotas, and into Minnesota. Temperatures fell 10 to 20 degrees in less than a couple hours as this front moved through the area. Light snow accompanied the front, but as winds increased, visibility dropped to less than one quarter of a mile. This was noted on several weather sensors, including the Minnesota Department of Transportation. Local webcams, and MNDOT mobile cameras also supported the blizzard conditions. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An arctic front associated with a strong trough digging into the Northern Plains brought a period of very strong winds, gusting as high as 40 to 50 mph, with a period of light snowfall. While accumulations were generally less than an inch, the areas with heavier snow from February 8-9 experienced an extended period of ground blizzard conditions from east central South Dakota into southwest Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusting to 40 to 50 mph at Marshall, when combined with new snowfall of one-half inch or less and the snowfall from February 8-9, produced visibility of around one-quarter mile for several hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a strong cold front moved across the Upper Midwest, dangerously cold wind chills occurred across portions of southeast Minnesota from the late evening of February 13th into the morning of the 14th. Wind chills of 30 to 40 below were common with the coldest values of 44 below occurring near Canton (Fillmore County) and in Rochester (Olmsted County). Several schools across the region utilized a two late start on the 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chills of 30 to 40 below zero were common across Olmsted County during the late evening of February 13th into the morning of the 14th. The coldest recorded wind chill was 44 below at Rochester. Several schools across the county used a two hour late start.

A potent storm system brought multiple rounds of severe storms to the Northland starting during the early morning hours of the 8th. Very little in the way of severe weather was observed with this initial round of storms. A warm front then lifted across the region through the day, bringing a very moist and unstable airmass to the area. Storms begin firing during the afternoon hours ahead of a cold front bringing all modes of severe weather during the late afternoon and evening hours before transitioning to more of a low end hail and damaging wind threat through the overnight hours and into the morning of the 9th. Numerous reports of hail and wind damage were received along with one confirmed tornado near Brainerd that was on the ground for nearly 25 minutes before lifting, but caused little in the way of damage.

With one to four inches of liquid residing in the snowpack near and north of Interstate 90 at the start of the month, a more or less ideal melting of the winter snowpack in early March produced flooding across southwest Minnesota rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The West Fork of the Des Moines River near Avoca (gage site AVOM5) rose above flood stage on March 4, and crested 1.71 feet above flood stage on March 6.  A significant amount of agricultural land was flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A bowing line of severe thunderstorms tracked from western into central North Dakota during the evening of Tuesday, July 7th. Slightly after midnight CDT, July 8th, storms out ahead of the main bowing segment entered the Barnes/Griggs county area. Eventually the main bowing segment tracked through Griggs and Steele counties before weakening. North of the main bowing segment, strong winds also tracked through portions of Benson and Ramsey counties. After 3 am CDT, a second bowing line of storms took shape over Richland County, North Dakota, which tracked through Otter Tail County, Minnesota. This segment also produced very strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning ignited a house fire, destroying the home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening of Sunday, June 28th, a complex of thunderstorms that developed across Iowa previously, moved slowly northward across southern Minnesota before stalling in central Minnesota Monday morning. Before the torrential rain began in south central Minnesota, a few of the storms caused minor damage to a home in Sibley County. ||Deep moisture convergence along the axis of thunderstorms, training along the periphery of the the numerous boundaries, combined with PWAT values of 1.7 to 1.9 inches which is over the 99th percentile value, caused extreme rainfall rates. ||There were two specific areas that the storms trained for a few hours and caused rainfall amounts of 4 of 8 inches. The first started around midnight just north of the Minnesota River Valley from Olivia, east-southeast to Winthrop, and Gaylord. Based on the return period from precipitation amounts in a specific time frame, the values were a 100-200 year event. Flash flood guidance values were 2 to 3 times above normal. As this area began to diminish, another area formed southeast of this area. The main area that received rainfall rates as high as 2 inches per hour occurred from southwest of New Ulm, northeast to St. Peter, and south of Montgomery. Other areas also received very heavy rainfall amounts in the south and southeast part of the Twin Cities metro area where locally 3 to 6 inches fell, with the heavier amounts of 6.04 inches over Afton, 5.55 inches near Burnsville, and 5.43 inches over Cottage Grove. ||As the runoff continued through the morning, the flash flood event become more areal flooding as the flood waters receded into creeks, streams, and eventually the mainstem rivers. ||Some of the highest rainfall totals around the area are as follows.||7 miles south of Bird Island, 7.56 inches. |5 miles north of Lake Crystal, 7.45 inches.|3 miles southwest of Kasota, 6.74 inches. |4 miles north of Miesville, 6.37 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a 12 hour rainfall report near Lakeville of 5.40 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across Minnesota during the afternoon of Thursday, June 18th. Modest instability and ample wind shear of 30 to 40 knots produced a line of thunderstorms across Minnesota. Most of storms were non-severe, but one storm produced damage northeast of Hutchinson where trees were blown down and blocked a road.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blocking the road near the intersection of Lake Hook Road, and 235th Street, and along County Road 7 and 230th Street. Power outages were reported in townships around Hutchinson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of a thunderstorm complex that produced flash flooding earlier across parts of southern Minnesota, and western Wisconsin, had reformed over central Minnesota during the evening of Monday, June 29th. A nearly stationary band of thunderstorms that was producing rainfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour, held over parts of Morrison County for several hours. There were several reports of rainfall amounts over 7 inches around Little Falls, with the highest total of 9.08 inches just southwest of the city. The city of Little Falls had many roads and culverts washed out, with some structures receiving water.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Torrential rainfall with measured amounts of over 9 inches occurred in the city of Little Falls, causing numerous roads, city and county, to close. The extent of the closed roads occurred several miles west and east of the city of Little Falls along Highway 27. The city of Little Falls had many roads and culverts washed out, with some structures receiving water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon of Tuesday, June 9th, a broken line of thunderstorms developed from Redwood Falls, northeast to Willmar, and Little Falls. These storms developed along a sharp boundary where winds shifted from northwest to southeast in a small area.||Although instability wasn't strong, there was enough wind shear in the lower layer of the atmosphere to cause the storms to rotate. Once these storms had adequate rotation, a few developed funnel clouds and one storm dropped a tornado north of Danube in an open field. No damage was reported but it was captured on video for a few minutes which lifted dust from the farm field.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a brief tornado touchdown in an open field north of Danube. The tornado was caught on video and pictures as it kicked up dust from the farm field, but toward the end, it did hit a few trees and break some large branches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Storms initiated over North Dakota Friday afternoon and congealed into a large storm complex that marched across Minnesota that evening and into the early morning hours. Damaging winds from the storm complex led to downed trees and minor structural damage in parts of north-central and northeast Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were downed by strong winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms formed along a cold front, which tracked from the northern Red River Valley through the northwest quarter of Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind gust was measured by a RAWS mesonet sensor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The summer of 2020 had more hot days than 2019, and July 17, 2020 was one of those hot days, with the exception of the Lake of the Woods region. Afternoon high temperatures ranged in the 80s to low 90s, with dew points in the mid 60s to mid 70s. During the early afternoon, a line of thunderstorms over north central North Dakota moved into the Devils Lake region. By the early evening, showers and storms covered most of the area north of Interstate 94 in North Dakota, and highway 10 in Minnesota. By mid to late evening, these showers and storms continued to sag southward, covering the rest of eastern North Dakota and west central Minnesota. These storms produced large hail and damaging wind gusts. In addition, most areas received from 1 to 4 inches of rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds damaged a porch.

A bowing line of severe thunderstorms tracked from western into central North Dakota during the evening of Tuesday, July 7th. Slightly after midnight CDT, July 8th, storms out ahead of the main bowing segment entered the Barnes/Griggs county area. Eventually the main bowing segment tracked through Griggs and Steele counties before weakening. North of the main bowing segment, strong winds also tracked through portions of Benson and Ramsey counties. After 3 am CDT, a second bowing line of storms took shape over Richland County, North Dakota, which tracked through Otter Tail County, Minnesota. This segment also produced very strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous tree branches were blown down in shelterbelts across Champion Township. A few large trees were also blown over.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A sharp cold front lied across far southern Minnesota during the afternoon of Tuesday, June 2nd. Temperatures ahead of this front rose into the mid to upper 90s with 70s and 80s behind it. In addition, dew points dropped significantly behind the front across central Minnesota. ||During the late morning, and early afternoon, elevated convection developed across central Minnesota and moved eastward but continued to develop on the southwest flank. Numerous hail stones were reported, but most were sub-severe. A few stronger updrafts contributed to a few hail stones as large as golf ball size near Alexandria. As the storms moved farther to the southeast, reports of large hail was observed near Elk River. ||By the mid-late afternoon, severe thunderstorms developed along a cold front across far southern Minnesota. Initially, most of the reports were very large hail, south of the Minnesota River, from around Lake Crystal, Garden City, Vernon Center and Good Thunder. There was a measured hail stone of 2.5 inches in diameter near Garden City. There was another measured report of 2.0 inches near Waldorf.  ||All of the significant wind damage reports were from the areas around New Richland, Waldorf, Hollandale, Myrtle, Albert Lea, Lewisville, and other areas of south central Minnesota due to severe thunderstorm outflow winds.  The storms were |highly visible from miles away and dozens of people took pictures and video.
