EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads were underwater across northwest Blue Earth County, including 206th, 105th, and 494th. A mudslide occurred at Highway 68 and County Road 42 at Judson, it was the result of the water flowing out of a drainage ditch/creek, 1/2 mile south on 42 that overflowed its banks where it turns and crosses County Road 42. It rushed through corn and hay fields carrying mud, parts of trees and deep fast moving water. On the west side it knocked flat a swath of shoulder high corn to pool along 68 and 42 because the culvert under hwy 68 couldn't handle the flow. There were other roads across northern and western parts of Blue Earth County, but the flooding was not as severe.

Storms initiated over North Dakota Friday afternoon and congealed into a large storm complex that marched across Minnesota that evening and into the early morning hours. Damaging winds from the storm complex led to downed trees and minor structural damage in parts of north-central and northeast Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees 10 to 12 inches in diameter were snapped or blown down during the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Friday, July 24th, was one of the hottest and most humid days of 2020. Afternoon high temperatures ranged from the upper 80s to middle 90s, with dew points in the middle 70s to lower 80s. During the early evening, thunderstorms broke out along two lines, one from Bismarck to northwest of Grand Forks, and the other from Bismarck to west of Fargo. By the late evening, the strongest storms along both lines were converging toward Grand Forks. This brought a period of very strong winds to eastern Grand Forks and eastern Walsh counties, continuing up toward the Lake of the Woods region. Meanwhile, another cluster of storms over south central North Dakota moved east-northeast, tracking through the Fargo-Moorhead area and into west central Minnesota during the overnight hours. These storms also produced some strong wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind gust was measured by a RWIS mesonet sensor along highway 2, near Mallory.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With one to four inches of liquid residing in the snowpack near and north of Interstate 90 at the start of the month, a more or less ideal melting of the winter snowpack in early March produced flooding across southwest Minnesota rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Redwood River at Russell (gage site RUSM5) crested 0.46 feet above flood stage on March 9. Minor flooding of agricultural land was the main impact.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A bowing line of severe thunderstorms tracked from western into central North Dakota during the evening of Tuesday, July 7th. Slightly after midnight CDT, July 8th, storms out ahead of the main bowing segment entered the Barnes/Griggs county area. Eventually the main bowing segment tracked through Griggs and Steele counties before weakening. North of the main bowing segment, strong winds also tracked through portions of Benson and Ramsey counties. After 3 am CDT, a second bowing line of storms took shape over Richland County, North Dakota, which tracked through Otter Tail County, Minnesota. This segment also produced very strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple wind gusts during this time frame were measured by a personal weather station.

During the evening of Sunday, June 28th, a complex of thunderstorms that developed across Iowa previously, moved slowly northward across southern Minnesota before stalling in central Minnesota Monday morning. Before the torrential rain began in south central Minnesota, a few of the storms caused minor damage to a home in Sibley County. ||Deep moisture convergence along the axis of thunderstorms, training along the periphery of the the numerous boundaries, combined with PWAT values of 1.7 to 1.9 inches which is over the 99th percentile value, caused extreme rainfall rates. ||There were two specific areas that the storms trained for a few hours and caused rainfall amounts of 4 of 8 inches. The first started around midnight just north of the Minnesota River Valley from Olivia, east-southeast to Winthrop, and Gaylord. Based on the return period from precipitation amounts in a specific time frame, the values were a 100-200 year event. Flash flood guidance values were 2 to 3 times above normal. As this area began to diminish, another area formed southeast of this area. The main area that received rainfall rates as high as 2 inches per hour occurred from southwest of New Ulm, northeast to St. Peter, and south of Montgomery. Other areas also received very heavy rainfall amounts in the south and southeast part of the Twin Cities metro area where locally 3 to 6 inches fell, with the heavier amounts of 6.04 inches over Afton, 5.55 inches near Burnsville, and 5.43 inches over Cottage Grove. ||As the runoff continued through the morning, the flash flood event become more areal flooding as the flood waters receded into creeks, streams, and eventually the mainstem rivers. ||Some of the highest rainfall totals around the area are as follows.||7 miles south of Bird Island, 7.56 inches. |5 miles north of Lake Crystal, 7.45 inches.|3 miles southwest of Kasota, 6.74 inches. |4 miles north of Miesville, 6.37 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter noted that a few trees were blown down around her farm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A sharp cold front lied across far southern Minnesota during the afternoon of Tuesday, June 2nd. Temperatures ahead of this front rose into the mid to upper 90s with 70s and 80s behind it. In addition, dew points dropped significantly behind the front across central Minnesota. ||During the late morning, and early afternoon, elevated convection developed across central Minnesota and moved eastward but continued to develop on the southwest flank. Numerous hail stones were reported, but most were sub-severe. A few stronger updrafts contributed to a few hail stones as large as golf ball size near Alexandria. As the storms moved farther to the southeast, reports of large hail was observed near Elk River. ||By the mid-late afternoon, severe thunderstorms developed along a cold front across far southern Minnesota. Initially, most of the reports were very large hail, south of the Minnesota River, from around Lake Crystal, Garden City, Vernon Center and Good Thunder. There was a measured hail stone of 2.5 inches in diameter near Garden City. There was another measured report of 2.0 inches near Waldorf.  ||All of the significant wind damage reports were from the areas around New Richland, Waldorf, Hollandale, Myrtle, Albert Lea, Lewisville, and other areas of south central Minnesota due to severe thunderstorm outflow winds.  The storms were |highly visible from miles away and dozens of people took pictures and video.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This was a measured wind gust from a storm chaser's wind sensor equipment.

Friday, July 24th, was one of the hottest and most humid days of 2020. Afternoon high temperatures ranged from the upper 80s to middle 90s, with dew points in the middle 70s to lower 80s. During the early evening, thunderstorms broke out along two lines, one from Bismarck to northwest of Grand Forks, and the other from Bismarck to west of Fargo. By the late evening, the strongest storms along both lines were converging toward Grand Forks. This brought a period of very strong winds to eastern Grand Forks and eastern Walsh counties, continuing up toward the Lake of the Woods region. Meanwhile, another cluster of storms over south central North Dakota moved east-northeast, tracking through the Fargo-Moorhead area and into west central Minnesota during the overnight hours. These storms also produced some strong wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An apple tree was blown down on the south side of Big Cormorant Lake. After the storm had passed, 1.58 inches of rain was also measured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms formed over the northern Red River Valley during the early morning hours of Saturday, July 4th. A couple of the individual storm cells became fairly strong, but the main hazard shifted to heavy rain. The storms were slow moving, but kept back-building, which led to some areas picking up very heavy rainfall. The northern Valley into extreme northwest Minnesota was already saturated from heavy rain during June, which led to flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime to quarter sized hail and heavy rain fell across western Foldahl Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Portions of northern Minnesota have seen rainfall deficits in excess of 5 inches for the year and by July areas of Severe Drought (D2) were observed across parts of the area. A wet middle part of July led to improving conditions that brought all areas into the Moderate Drought (D1) category by the end of the month, however, rainfall deficits persist and further dry conditions may reverse the improving trend.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions worsened at the beginning of July as rainfall deficits for the year approached 6 to 8 inches. A few rainfall events during the middle of the month resulted in total rainfall for the month in the 2 to 4 inch range, which was still below normal. However, it was enough to lift the area out of the Severe Drought category (D2), especially with the rainfall received on July 21, when around 1 to 1.50 inches fell.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions worsened at the beginning of July as rainfall deficits for the year approached 5 to 7 inches. A few rainfall events during the middle of the month resulted in total rainfall for the month in the 3 to 5.50 inch range, with Duluth seeing totals about 1.50 inches above normal. This was enough to lift the area out of the Severe Drought category (D2), especially with the rainfall received on July 18 and 21, when around 1 to 2 inches total fell.

However, it was enough to lift the area out of the Severe Drought category (D2), especially with the rainfall received on July 18 and 21, when around 1 to 2 inches fell total.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent storm system brought multiple rounds of severe storms to the Northland starting during the early morning hours of the 8th. Very little in the way of severe weather was observed with this initial round of storms. A warm front then lifted across the region through the day, bringing a very moist and unstable airmass to the area. Storms begin firing during the afternoon hours ahead of a cold front bringing all modes of severe weather during the late afternoon and evening hours before transitioning to more of a low end hail and damaging wind threat through the overnight hours and into the morning of the 9th. Numerous reports of hail and wind damage were received along with one confirmed tornado near Brainerd that was on the ground for nearly 25 minutes before lifting, but caused little in the way of damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An 8 inch diameter oak tree was uprooted by the winds during the storm. The tree then fell onto a fence causing damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Friday, July 24th, was one of the hottest and most humid days of 2020. Afternoon high temperatures ranged from the upper 80s to middle 90s, with dew points in the middle 70s to lower 80s. During the early evening, thunderstorms broke out along two lines, one from Bismarck to northwest of Grand Forks, and the other from Bismarck to west of Fargo. By the late evening, the strongest storms along both lines were converging toward Grand Forks. This brought a period of very strong winds to eastern Grand Forks and eastern Walsh counties, continuing up toward the Lake of the Woods region. Meanwhile, another cluster of storms over south central North Dakota moved east-northeast, tracking through the Fargo-Moorhead area and into west central Minnesota during the overnight hours. These storms also produced some strong wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were blown down between Kabekona and Benedict lakes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The summer of 2020 had more hot days than 2019, and July 17, 2020 was one of those hot days, with the exception of the Lake of the Woods region. Afternoon high temperatures ranged in the 80s to low 90s, with dew points in the mid 60s to mid 70s. During the early afternoon, a line of thunderstorms over north central North Dakota moved into the Devils Lake region. By the early evening, showers and storms covered most of the area north of Interstate 94 in North Dakota, and highway 10 in Minnesota. By mid to late evening, these showers and storms continued to sag southward, covering the rest of eastern North Dakota and west central Minnesota. These storms produced large hail and damaging wind gusts. In addition, most areas received from 1 to 4 inches of rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roofing was torn off a trailer home.

A cluster of thunderstorms formed over the northern Red River Valley during the early morning hours of Saturday, July 4th. A couple of the individual storm cells became fairly strong, but the main hazard shifted to heavy rain. The storms were slow moving, but kept back-building, which led to some areas picking up very heavy rainfall. The northern Valley into extreme northwest Minnesota was already saturated from heavy rain during June, which led to flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail fell with very heavy rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening of Sunday, June 28th, a complex of thunderstorms that developed across Iowa previously, moved slowly northward across southern Minnesota before stalling in central Minnesota Monday morning. Before the torrential rain began in south central Minnesota, a few of the storms caused minor damage to a home in Sibley County. ||Deep moisture convergence along the axis of thunderstorms, training along the periphery of the the numerous boundaries, combined with PWAT values of 1.7 to 1.9 inches which is over the 99th percentile value, caused extreme rainfall rates. ||There were two specific areas that the storms trained for a few hours and caused rainfall amounts of 4 of 8 inches. The first started around midnight just north of the Minnesota River Valley from Olivia, east-southeast to Winthrop, and Gaylord. Based on the return period from precipitation amounts in a specific time frame, the values were a 100-200 year event. Flash flood guidance values were 2 to 3 times above normal. As this area began to diminish, another area formed southeast of this area. The main area that received rainfall rates as high as 2 inches per hour occurred from southwest of New Ulm, northeast to St. Peter, and south of Montgomery. Other areas also received very heavy rainfall amounts in the south and southeast part of the Twin Cities metro area where locally 3 to 6 inches fell, with the heavier amounts of 6.04 inches over Afton, 5.55 inches near Burnsville, and 5.43 inches over Cottage Grove. ||As the runoff continued through the morning, the flash flood event become more areal flooding as the flood waters receded into creeks, streams, and eventually the mainstem rivers. ||Some of the highest rainfall totals around the area are as follows.||7 miles south of Bird Island, 7.56 inches. |5 miles north of Lake Crystal, 7.45 inches.|3 miles southwest of Kasota, 6.74 inches. |4 miles north of Miesville, 6.37 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several local officials and trained spotters indicated the excessive rainfall caused numerous roads to flood between Gibbon, Winthrop, and Gaylord. Flood waters came out of the middle branch of the Rush River, and over the golf course in Winthrop. Even as the flood waters receded several roads were closed or partially reopened.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Portions of northern Minnesota have seen rainfall deficits in excess of 5 inches for the year and by July areas of Severe Drought (D2) were observed across parts of the area. A wet middle part of July led to improving conditions that brought all areas into the Moderate Drought (D1) category by the end of the month, however, rainfall deficits persist and further dry conditions may reverse the improving trend.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions worsened at the beginning of July as rainfall deficits for the year approached 6 to 8 inches. A few rainfall events during the middle of the month resulted in total rainfall for the month in the 1 to 3 inch range, which was still below normal. However, it was enough to lift the area out of the Severe Drought category (D2), especially with the rainfall received on July 18 and 21, when around 1 to 2 inches fell total.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moving through Manitoba into western Ontario during the day and pulled a cold front through Minnesota during the afternoon and evening hours. Strong southerly flow ahead of the front brought warm temperatures and moisture to the region to help fuel the storms that developed ahead of the cold front. While a line of storms did develop, afternoon cloud cover delayed their development and prevented the storms from being as strong as the environment would have supported. Only a few wind damage reports were received with the storms with the most significant being a pole barn that was completely destroyed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree roughly 4 inches in diameter was snapped by the winds and fell onto a trailer.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The summer of 2020 had more hot days than 2019, and July 17, 2020 was one of those hot days, with the exception of the Lake of the Woods region. Afternoon high temperatures ranged in the 80s to low 90s, with dew points in the mid 60s to mid 70s. During the early afternoon, a line of thunderstorms over north central North Dakota moved into the Devils Lake region. By the early evening, showers and storms covered most of the area north of Interstate 94 in North Dakota, and highway 10 in Minnesota. By mid to late evening, these showers and storms continued to sag southward, covering the rest of eastern North Dakota and west central Minnesota. These storms produced large hail and damaging wind gusts. In addition, most areas received from 1 to 4 inches of rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was snapped.

Storms begin firing during the afternoon hours ahead of a cold front bringing all modes of severe weather during the late afternoon and evening hours before transitioning to more of a low end hail and damaging wind threat through the overnight hours and into the morning of the 9th. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A supercell briefly produced a tornado in a rural area between Brainerd and Garrison. The tornado produced minor damage to one  property as well as damaging several trees along its path. The tornado was estimated to be on the ground for nearly 23 minutes |before dissipating as the parent storm became outflow dominant.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lone weak cell appeared on radar at 430 pm CDT over northern Grant County, Minnesota. Numerous storm chasers had congregated in this area, hoping to see a good storm to chase. By 5 pm, the cell had blossomed into a strong thunderstorm. Weather spotters, law enforcement, and storm chasers then reported a funnel coming down toward the ground slightly after 505 pm. A tornado warning was issued at 508 pm (until 6 pm) for portions of south central Otter Tail and northeast Grant counties. This tornado tracked about 9 miles through the warned area, before dissipating. At its peak strength, it was rated as an EF-4 tornado. Another rotation was noted a little later, leading to a second tornado warning at 635 pm (until 7 pm), this time for southwest Wadena and southeast Otter Tail counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A persistent funnel cloud briefly touched down. Peak winds were estimated at 75 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening of Sunday, June 28th, a complex of thunderstorms that developed across Iowa previously, moved slowly northward across southern Minnesota before stalling in central Minnesota Monday morning. Before the torrential rain began in south central Minnesota, a few of the storms caused minor damage to a home in Sibley County. ||Deep moisture convergence along the axis of thunderstorms, training along the periphery of the the numerous boundaries, combined with PWAT values of 1.7 to 1.9 inches which is over the 99th percentile value, caused extreme rainfall rates. ||There were two specific areas that the storms trained for a few hours and caused rainfall amounts of 4 of 8 inches. The first started around midnight just north of the Minnesota River Valley from Olivia, east-southeast to Winthrop, and Gaylord. Based on the return period from precipitation amounts in a specific time frame, the values were a 100-200 year event. Flash flood guidance values were 2 to 3 times above normal. As this area began to diminish, another area formed southeast of this area. The main area that received rainfall rates as high as 2 inches per hour occurred from southwest of New Ulm, northeast to St. Peter, and south of Montgomery. Other areas also received very heavy rainfall amounts in the south and southeast part of the Twin Cities metro area where locally 3 to 6 inches fell, with the heavier amounts of 6.04 inches over Afton, 5.55 inches near Burnsville, and 5.43 inches over Cottage Grove. ||As the runoff continued through the morning, the flash flood event become more areal flooding as the flood waters receded into creeks, streams, and eventually the mainstem rivers. ||Some of the highest rainfall totals around the area are as follows.||7 miles south of Bird Island, 7.56 inches. |5 miles north of Lake Crystal, 7.45 inches.|3 miles southwest of Kasota, 6.74 inches. |4 miles north of Miesville, 6.37 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a 12 hour rainfall report near Judson of 5.50 inches.

With one to four inches of liquid residing in the snowpack near and north of Interstate 90 at the start of the month, a more or less ideal melting of the winter snowpack in early March produced flooding across southwest Minnesota rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The West Fork of the Des Moines River at Windom (gage site WDOM5) rose above flood stage on March 6, and crested 0.77 feet above flood stage on March 10. The main impact was flooding of agricultural land.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Redwood River at Marshall (gage site MSHM5) rose briefly above flood stage and crested at 1.19 feet above flood stage on March 7. Minor flooding of agricultural areas occurred outside Marshall.

Combined with dew points in the middle 70s to lower 80s, heat indices over the Red River Valley rose into the 105 to 110 range during the afternoon and early evening.

During the evening of Sunday, June 28th, a complex of thunderstorms that developed across Iowa previously, moved slowly northward across southern Minnesota before stalling in central Minnesota Monday morning. Before the torrential rain began in south central Minnesota, a few of the storms caused minor damage to a home in Sibley County. ||Deep moisture convergence along the axis of thunderstorms, training along the periphery of the the numerous boundaries, combined with PWAT values of 1.7 to 1.9 inches which is over the 99th percentile value, caused extreme rainfall rates. ||There were two specific areas that the storms trained for a few hours and caused rainfall amounts of 4 of 8 inches. The first started around midnight just north of the Minnesota River Valley from Olivia, east-southeast to Winthrop, and Gaylord. Based on the return period from precipitation amounts in a specific time frame, the values were a 100-200 year event. Flash flood guidance values were 2 to 3 times above normal. As this area began to diminish, another area formed southeast of this area. The main area that received rainfall rates as high as 2 inches per hour occurred from southwest of New Ulm, northeast to St. Peter, and south of Montgomery. Other areas also received very heavy rainfall amounts in the south and southeast part of the Twin Cities metro area where locally 3 to 6 inches fell, with the heavier amounts of 6.04 inches over Afton, 5.55 inches near Burnsville, and 5.43 inches over Cottage Grove. ||As the runoff continued through the morning, the flash flood event become more areal flooding as the flood waters receded into creeks, streams, and eventually the mainstem rivers. ||Some of the highest rainfall totals around the area are as follows.||7 miles south of Bird Island, 7.56 inches. |5 miles north of Lake Crystal, 7.45 inches.|3 miles southwest of Kasota, 6.74 inches. |4 miles north of Miesville, 6.37 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were numerous reports of flooded or closed roadways due to water over the road across southern Nicollet County. There were multiple mud slides in the Minnesota River Valley, including one on Highway 169, south of St. Peter. There was also rushing water over 490th street at Highway 14, southeast of Nicollet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent storm system brought multiple rounds of severe storms to the Northland starting during the early morning hours of the 8th. Very little in the way of severe weather was observed with this initial round of storms. A warm front then lifted across the region through the day, bringing a very moist and unstable airmass to the area. Storms begin firing during the afternoon hours ahead of a cold front bringing all modes of severe weather during the late afternoon and evening hours before transitioning to more of a low end hail and damaging wind threat through the overnight hours and into the morning of the 9th. Numerous reports of hail and wind damage were received along with one confirmed tornado near Brainerd that was on the ground for nearly 25 minutes before lifting, but caused little in the way of damage.

Saturday, July 25th was another hot and humid day, with highs in the 80s to around 90, and dew points in the 70s. By late in the afternoon, a cold front had moved into the Devils Lake region, helping to initiate a few thunderstorms. One severe storm tracked across portions of western Cavalier County, dropping large hail. There was a bit of a lull in thunderstorm activity until around sundown, when storms flared up again between Jamestown, Grand Forks, and Devils Lake. After midnight, another batch of strong storms tracked along the North and South Dakota border, from Oakes (ND) toward Elbow Lake (MN). These storms produced large and and strong wind gusts as well.

A sharp cold front lied across far southern Minnesota during the afternoon of Tuesday, June 2nd. Temperatures ahead of this front rose into the mid to upper 90s with 70s and 80s behind it. In addition, dew points dropped significantly behind the front across central Minnesota. ||During the late morning, and early afternoon, elevated convection developed across central Minnesota and moved eastward but continued to develop on the southwest flank. Numerous hail stones were reported, but most were sub-severe. A few stronger updrafts contributed to a few hail stones as large as golf ball size near Alexandria. As the storms moved farther to the southeast, reports of large hail was observed near Elk River. ||By the mid-late afternoon, severe thunderstorms developed along a cold front across far southern Minnesota. Initially, most of the reports were very large hail, south of the Minnesota River, from around Lake Crystal, Garden City, Vernon Center and Good Thunder. There was a measured hail stone of 2.5 inches in diameter near Garden City. There was another measured report of 2.0 inches near Waldorf.  ||All of the significant wind damage reports were from the areas around New Richland, Waldorf, Hollandale, Myrtle, Albert Lea, Lewisville, and other areas of south central Minnesota due to severe thunderstorm outflow winds.  The storms were |highly visible from miles away and dozens of people took pictures and video.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A semi truck was overturned east of Albert Lea on County Road 21.

An elevated layer of instability occurred across central Minnesota during the morning of Monday, June 1st. Thunderstorms that developed east of Fargo moved eastward and produced a swath of large hail across central Minnesota from north of Fort Ripley, east to Isle and Warman, or across northern Mille Lacs and northern Kanabec counties. A lot of the larger hail probably fell in Lake Mille Lacs.

A complex of thunderstorms developed over the Dakotas the evening of the 24th and pushed eastward overnight into the morning of the 25th. While the storms were sub-severe for the most part, some stronger winds were able to reach the surface in the vicinity of Leech Lake in north-central Minnesota causing some tree damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were downed at the Red Wing Resort on Leech Lake by the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An elevated layer of instability occurred across central Minnesota during the morning of Monday, June 1st. Thunderstorms that developed east of Fargo moved eastward and produced a swath of large hail across central Minnesota from north of Fort Ripley, east to Isle and Warman, or across northern Mille Lacs and northern Kanabec counties. A lot of the larger hail probably fell in Lake Mille Lacs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were several reports of large hail, up to half dollar size, that fell in a swath from south of Vineland, southeast toward the Mille Lacs, and Kanabec County line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broken line of strong thunderstorms developed along a weak cold front across southwest, northeast toward the Twin Cities, and into west central Wisconsin Sunday afternoon, June 21st. These storms were mainly strong with numerous reports of pea to occasionally dime size hail stones. A couple of storms briefly produced up to half dollar size hail during the afternoon. Gusty winds also accompany the storms, but wind gusts were between 30 and 40 mph. A couple of wind gusts of 40 to 45 mph occurred near New Prague and Madelia in southern Minnesota. Other thunderstorms produced very heavy rainfall with amounts of 1 to 2 inches in several locations in southern Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large hail fell just south of the Martin, Watonwan County line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms in southeast North Dakota in the late evening of the 17th descended southeast across west central Minnesota.  The unique factor with these storms were the significant gust front winds that occurred well out ahead of the main area of thunderstorms. Severe wind gusts from 60 to 70 mph occurred with some damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Estimated seventy mph winds uprooted a large tree which fell onto a cabin with no injuries reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A bowing line of severe thunderstorms tracked from western into central North Dakota during the evening of Tuesday, July 7th. Slightly after midnight CDT, July 8th, storms out ahead of the main bowing segment entered the Barnes/Griggs county area. Eventually the main bowing segment tracked through Griggs and Steele counties before weakening. North of the main bowing segment, strong winds also tracked through portions of Benson and Ramsey counties. After 3 am CDT, a second bowing line of storms took shape over Richland County, North Dakota, which tracked through Otter Tail County, Minnesota. This segment also produced very strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado began in Wilkin County, but ended in Otter Tail County, on the north side of Fergus Falls. The tornado was wrapped in downburst winds and rain, with a total track length of about 11 miles. The tornado uprooted or snapped numerous trees in shelterbelts along its path. It also snapped a few power poles and tore the roof off and collapsed the walls at a welding and manufacturing shop near the Fergus Falls airport. Peak winds were estimated at 100 mph.

As the storms moved farther to the southeast, reports of large hail was observed near Elk River. ||By the mid-late afternoon, severe thunderstorms developed along a cold front across far southern Minnesota. Initially, most of the reports were very large hail, south of the Minnesota River, from around Lake Crystal, Garden City, Vernon Center and Good Thunder. There was a measured hail stone of 2.5 inches in diameter near Garden City. There was another measured report of 2.0 inches near Waldorf.  ||All of the significant wind damage reports were from the areas around New Richland, Waldorf, Hollandale, Myrtle, Albert Lea, Lewisville, and other areas of south central Minnesota due to severe thunderstorm outflow winds.  The storms were |highly visible from miles away and dozens of people took pictures and video.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Automated weather station TKC measured a peak wind gust of 74 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure and a cold front moved across the Northland on the afternoon and evening of the 18th. Ahead of the cold front, dew points surged into the 70s and, coupled with the cold front, led to the development of a few supercell thunderstorms that moved across the region. The storms developed over north-central Minnesota during the afternoon and pushed eastward through northwest Wisconsin during the evening hours producing damaging winds and large hail as they pushed across the area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado, wrapped in heavy rain and downburst winds, snapped multiple power poles at three points along its path. It also tossed calving sheds and flipped an irrigation system, while surrounding winds blew down trees in and around its path. Peak winds were estimated at 115 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lone weak cell appeared on radar at 430 pm CDT over northern Grant County, Minnesota. Numerous storm chasers had congregated in this area, hoping to see a good storm to chase. By 5 pm, the cell had blossomed into a strong thunderstorm. Weather spotters, law enforcement, and storm chasers then reported a funnel coming down toward the ground slightly after 505 pm. A tornado warning was issued at 508 pm (until 6 pm) for portions of south central Otter Tail and northeast Grant counties. This tornado tracked about 9 miles through the warned area, before dissipating. At its peak strength, it was rated as an EF-4 tornado. Another rotation was noted a little later, leading to a second tornado warning at 635 pm (until 7 pm), this time for southwest Wadena and southeast Otter Tail counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado began in Grant County, but after about two miles, it crossed into Otter Tail County, where it ended about 5 miles east of Dalton. The total track through both counties was about 9 miles. Peak winds were estimated at 170 mph, based on available damage indicators. At least 10 farmsteads were impacted, with three homes, one pole shed, and one machine shed destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With deep moisture resulting in around 1.75 inches precipitable water, slow-moving thunderstorms along a cold front brought 2.74 inches of rainfall at Worthington during the afternoon of June 18.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure and a cold front moved across the Northland on the afternoon and evening of the 18th. Ahead of the cold front, dew points surged into the 70s and, coupled with the cold front, led to the development of a few supercell thunderstorms that moved across the region. The storms developed over north-central Minnesota during the afternoon and pushed eastward through northwest Wisconsin during the evening hours producing damaging winds and large hail as they pushed across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Eight large trees were downed across County Road 4 and Vermilion Trail.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two aspen trees 6 inches in diameter were downed by the winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lone weak cell appeared on radar at 430 pm CDT over northern Grant County, Minnesota. Numerous storm chasers had congregated in this area, hoping to see a good storm to chase. By 5 pm, the cell had blossomed into a strong thunderstorm. Weather spotters, law enforcement, and storm chasers then reported a funnel coming down toward the ground slightly after 505 pm. A tornado warning was issued at 508 pm (until 6 pm) for portions of south central Otter Tail and northeast Grant counties. This tornado tracked about 9 miles through the warned area, before dissipating. At its peak strength, it was rated as an EF-4 tornado. Another rotation was noted a little later, leading to a second tornado warning at 635 pm (until 7 pm), this time for southwest Wadena and southeast Otter Tail counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado began in Grant County, but after about two miles, it crossed into Otter Tail County, where it ended about 5 miles east of Dalton. The total track through both counties was about 9 miles. Peak winds were estimated at 170 mph, based on available damage indicators. At least 10 farmsteads were impacted, with three homes, one pole shed, and one machine shed destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early afternoon of July 11th, isolated thunderstorms formed between Langdon (ND), Hillsboro (ND), and Alexandria (MN). One of these storms produced a tornado over southwest Becker County (MN), southwest of Detroit Lakes, Minnesota.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A rope tornado meandered southward for about 1.5 miles, tracking through a wind farm area with a mix of pastureland and gravel quarries. At an abandoned farmstead, it tore more of the roof off a barn and roofing panels off other farm buildings. Peak winds were estimated at 95 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The summer of 2020 had more hot days than 2019, and July 17, 2020 was one of those hot days, with the exception of the Lake of the Woods region. Afternoon high temperatures ranged in the 80s to low 90s, with dew points in the mid 60s to mid 70s. During the early afternoon, a line of thunderstorms over north central North Dakota moved into the Devils Lake region. By the early evening, showers and storms covered most of the area north of Interstate 94 in North Dakota, and highway 10 in Minnesota. By mid to late evening, these showers and storms continued to sag southward, covering the rest of eastern North Dakota and west central Minnesota. These storms produced large hail and damaging wind gusts. In addition, most areas received from 1 to 4 inches of rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado was partially embedded in downburst winds and heavy rain. It tracked through one farmyard and across the town of Argyle, and ended about 1 mile east of town. Grain bins were crumpled, trees snapped, and shingles torn off roofs. In Argyle, a tall grain bin was caved in and its top conveyor was torn off at the elevator complex, and the bleachers were tossed at the baseball field.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A sharp cold front lied across far southern Minnesota during the afternoon of Tuesday, June 2nd. Temperatures ahead of this front rose into the mid to upper 90s with 70s and 80s behind it. In addition, dew points dropped significantly behind the front across central Minnesota. ||During the late morning, and early afternoon, elevated convection developed across central Minnesota and moved eastward but continued to develop on the southwest flank. Numerous hail stones were reported, but most were sub-severe. A few stronger updrafts contributed to a few hail stones as large as golf ball size near Alexandria. As the storms moved farther to the southeast, reports of large hail was observed near Elk River. ||By the mid-late afternoon, severe thunderstorms developed along a cold front across far southern Minnesota. Initially, most of the reports were very large hail, south of the Minnesota River, from around Lake Crystal, Garden City, Vernon Center and Good Thunder. There was a measured hail stone of 2.5 inches in diameter near Garden City. There was another measured report of 2.0 inches near Waldorf.  ||All of the significant wind damage reports were from the areas around New Richland, Waldorf, Hollandale, Myrtle, Albert Lea, Lewisville, and other areas of south central Minnesota due to severe thunderstorm outflow winds.  The storms were |highly visible from miles away and dozens of people took pictures and video.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A shed and home were damaged in Hollandale. There was also several power poles blown down around the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent storm system brought multiple rounds of severe storms to the Northland starting during the early morning hours of the 8th. Very little in the way of severe weather was observed with this initial round of storms. A warm front then lifted across the region through the day, bringing a very moist and unstable airmass to the area. Storms begin firing during the afternoon hours ahead of a cold front bringing all modes of severe weather during the late afternoon and evening hours before transitioning to more of a low end hail and damaging wind threat through the overnight hours and into the morning of the 9th. Numerous reports of hail and wind damage were received along with one confirmed tornado near Brainerd that was on the ground for nearly 25 minutes before lifting, but caused little in the way of damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed by the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Friday, July 24th, was one of the hottest and most humid days of 2020. Afternoon high temperatures ranged from the upper 80s to middle 90s, with dew points in the middle 70s to lower 80s. During the early evening, thunderstorms broke out along two lines, one from Bismarck to northwest of Grand Forks, and the other from Bismarck to west of Fargo. By the late evening, the strongest storms along both lines were converging toward Grand Forks. This brought a period of very strong winds to eastern Grand Forks and eastern Walsh counties, continuing up toward the Lake of the Woods region. Meanwhile, another cluster of storms over south central North Dakota moved east-northeast, tracking through the Fargo-Moorhead area and into west central Minnesota during the overnight hours. These storms also produced some strong wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak tornado formed in the bookend vortex portion of a squall line. The tornado knocked down sunflowers and left a distinctive swirl pattern. Peak winds were estimated at 65 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Friday, July 24th, was one of the hottest and most humid days of 2020. Afternoon high temperatures ranged from the upper 80s to middle 90s, with dew points in the middle 70s to lower 80s. During the early evening, thunderstorms broke out along two lines, one from Bismarck to northwest of Grand Forks, and the other from Bismarck to west of Fargo. By the late evening, the strongest storms along both lines were converging toward Grand Forks. Meanwhile, another cluster of storms over south central North Dakota moved east-northeast, tracking through the Fargo-Moorhead area and into west central Minnesota during the overnight hours. These storms also produced some strong wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A microburst (downburst wind) likely initiated over the west end of Long Lake and moved rapidly eastward across Long Lake Point Road. A trailer home was blown over and demolished. Several large tree limbs were broken down by the wind, while a pontoon was flipped over by the combination of wave and wind action.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With deep moisture resulting in around 1.75 inches precipitable water, slow-moving thunderstorms along a cold front brought 2.74 inches of rainfall at Worthington during the afternoon of June 18.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One vehicle became stalled in high water, with six reports of street flooding in the city. Property damage costs are rough estimates.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small complex of storms that developed across central Minnesota around sunrise, moved southeast toward St. Cloud, Minnesota. Ample instability and a stronger updraft caused an isolated severe storm near the St. Cloud Airport where a trained spotter observed quarter size hail.

A small complex of storms that moved out of northeast South Dakota late Saturday morning caused a concentrated area of straight-line wind damage. The Canby, Minnesota airport measured a wind gust of 72 mph. These type of wind speeds continued across portions of Yellow Medicine and Lac Qui Parle counties where hundreds of trees and power lines were blown down. There was another wind gust of 68 mph at the Department of Transportation road sensor (Near Hanley Falls, MN) that went offline as the gust was recorded. ||The worst area occurred in the city of Granite Falls where winds tore open two roofs and allowed water to pour through ventilation ducts into classrooms and hallways, causing hundreds of thousands of dollars in damage to the Yellow Medicine East School District building. The Granite Fun restaurant and golf course also sustained damage with 55 to 60 trees blown down. Due to the extensive damage, the city of Granite Falls declared a state of emergency. ||Although wind damage occurred east of Granite Falls, mainly between Sacred Heart to Renville, this storm weakened as it moved eastward toward the western part of the Twin Cities.||A secondary area of storms developed across south central Minnesota during the mid-late afternoon near New Ulm, and produced another area of significant wind damage eastward toward Cannon Falls, and Red Wing. There was a swath of tree damage around Cannon Falls where one house lost its roof, with other having minor roof damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon of Friday, August, 14th, thunderstorms developed along a warm front across central Minnesota, with additional development southward in the warm sector by the late afternoon, and early evening. These storms were able to produce many tornadoes, most of which were rated EF-0, but three were rated EF-1. In addition to the tornadoes, there were other reports of downburst wind damage throughout central and southern Minnesota. ||Over a two week period, there were numerous road trips to determine if tornadoes occurred in rural areas. Due to the several storm spotters, emergency managers, and those who had drones to further the investigation, plus individuals recording the brief touchdowns, there were 16 tornadoes that occurred in Chanhassen NWS forecast area. Most of the tornadoes were confined to rural farmland, but there were a few that did damage farms, sheds, and outbuildings.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This is the continuation of the Kandiyohi tornado that moved into Meeker County near 340th Street and 210th Street Southeast. The tornado continued to the northeast damaging many trees and corn/bean fields before dissipating near Crow River. It moved across one farmstead but only produced minor damage to outbuildings. At one point, a law enforcement officer along the Meeker/Kandiyohi line reported and videod one tornado to his south and another to his east. This tornado was the one to his east.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS survey indicated that an EF-0 tornado tracked over mostly fields and open land, but it did hit some trees, downing large tree branches. Downburst wind damage was noted adjacent to the tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening of Sunday, August 9th, a storm developed in Carver County, west of the NWS office. Several more storms developed and trained over the same areas that affected eastern Carver, far northern Scott, and southern Hennepin Counties for an hour. The storms were difficult to recognize the severity at first as they developed nearly overhead from the radar location in Chanhassen.  ||Once the storms weakened over southern Hennepin County, more storms reformed northwest of the Twin Cities, and again produced very large hail around Buffalo, Montrose, Hanover, Rockford, and Loretto.  These storms propagated east-southeast toward the western suburbs of the Twin Cities and produced damaging hail between Lake Minnetonka, Eden Prairie and Edina. Slowly, the storms began to propagate to the southeast across south-central Minnesota, by the early morning. ||There was a period where hailstones were as large as baseballs (based on pictures from social media) across the western metro area. The two areas that had pictures of the largest stones were Loretto and Victoria.||Based on social media pictures, radar, and MRMS information, there were four areas that likely had hail up to baseball size hail, or possibly greater. The first area was the southern part of Kanabec County. The second area was from southeast Wright County, eastward across Hennepin between Lake Minnetonka to Edina. The third area was near Jordan, southeast to Lydia in Scott County. The fourth area was along the Dakota, Rice County line near Northfield.||Another unique part of these storms is that the hail was nearly continuous for 5 to 15 minutes, and some of the larger hailstones caused significant damage to cars and roofs.||In addition to all of the hail reports, several lines of thunderstorms that continued over the same areas, produced a wide area of very heavy rainfall reports with the heaviest at the NWS office at 5.53 inches. Reports between two and four inches that weren't included in the event were concentrated between Victoria, Chanhassen, Shakopee, Eden Prairie, Bloomington and Lake Minnetonka.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were a few reports of large hail, the largest measuring golf ball size, occurred west of Hanover, to the community of Hanover, and moved eastward over the Wright, Hennepin County line.

Based on radar, and observations, there was a swath of hail between Buffalo, and Montrose, including the towns, that moved eastward toward the Hennepin County line near Rockford. There was also likely large hail west of Buffalo but due to the time of night and the rural area, there were no reports and is not included here.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small complex of storms that moved out of northeast South Dakota late Saturday morning caused a concentrated area of straight-line wind damage. The Canby, Minnesota airport measured a wind gust of 72 mph. These type of wind speeds continued across portions of Yellow Medicine and Lac Qui Parle counties where hundreds of trees and power lines were blown down. There was another wind gust of 68 mph at the Department of Transportation road sensor (Near Hanley Falls, MN) that went offline as the gust was recorded. ||The worst area occurred in the city of Granite Falls where winds tore open two roofs and allowed water to pour through ventilation ducts into classrooms and hallways, causing hundreds of thousands of dollars in damage to the Yellow Medicine East School District building. The Granite Fun restaurant and golf course also sustained damage with 55 to 60 trees blown down. Due to the extensive damage, the city of Granite Falls declared a state of emergency. ||Although wind damage occurred east of Granite Falls, mainly between Sacred Heart to Renville, this storm weakened as it moved eastward toward the western part of the Twin Cities.||A secondary area of storms developed across south central Minnesota during the mid-late afternoon near New Ulm, and produced another area of significant wind damage eastward toward Cannon Falls, and Red Wing. There was a swath of tree damage around Cannon Falls where one house lost its roof, with other having minor roof damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon of Friday, August, 14th, thunderstorms developed along a warm front across central Minnesota, with additional development southward in the warm sector by the late afternoon, and early evening. These storms were able to produce many tornadoes, most of which were rated EF-0, but three were rated EF-1. In addition to the tornadoes, there were other reports of downburst wind damage throughout central and southern Minnesota. ||Over a two week period, there were numerous road trips to determine if tornadoes occurred in rural areas. Due to the several storm spotters, emergency managers, and those who had drones to further the investigation, plus individuals recording the brief touchdowns, there were 16 tornadoes that occurred in Chanhassen NWS forecast area. Most of the tornadoes were confined to rural farmland, but there were a few that did damage farms, sheds, and outbuildings.

A few thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front in north central Minnesota during the evening hours and pushed eastward into the overnight hours. One of the storms produced marginally severe hail and a few funnel clouds over Cass County while others produced scattered wind and hail damage along the I-35 corridor during the early morning hours and around sunrise.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was observed just above the tree tops and exhibited strong rotation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small complex of storms that moved out of northeast South Dakota late Saturday morning caused a concentrated area of straight-line wind damage. The Canby, Minnesota airport measured a wind gust of 72 mph. These type of wind speeds continued across portions of Yellow Medicine and Lac Qui Parle counties where hundreds of trees and power lines were blown down. There was another wind gust of 68 mph at the Department of Transportation road sensor (Near Hanley Falls, MN) that went offline as the gust was recorded. ||The worst area occurred in the city of Granite Falls where winds tore open two roofs and allowed water to pour through ventilation ducts into classrooms and hallways, causing hundreds of thousands of dollars in damage to the Yellow Medicine East School District building. The Granite Fun restaurant and golf course also sustained damage with 55 to 60 trees blown down. Due to the extensive damage, the city of Granite Falls declared a state of emergency. ||Although wind damage occurred east of Granite Falls, mainly between Sacred Heart to Renville, this storm weakened as it moved eastward toward the western part of the Twin Cities.||A secondary area of storms developed across south central Minnesota during the mid-late afternoon near New Ulm, and produced another area of significant wind damage eastward toward Cannon Falls, and Red Wing. There was a swath of tree damage around Cannon Falls where one house lost its roof, with other having minor roof damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was lots of wind damage in Red Wing where many trees and branches were blown down, including damage to roofs, and power outages throughout the city. There was a measured wind report from a private weather station of 61 mph west of Red Wing at 1635 LST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of storms that originated over southern North Dakota Sunday evening, August 23rd, moved over west-central Minnesota during the early morning and caused significant wind gusts, including at the Benson Airport. Some damage was noted around Benson as 84 MPH winds gusted at the airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large trees and power lines/poles were blown down around Benson, Minnesota.

During the afternoon of Friday, August, 14th, thunderstorms developed along a warm front across central Minnesota, with additional development southward in the warm sector by the late afternoon, and early evening. These storms were able to produce many tornadoes, most of which were rated EF-0, but three were rated EF-1. In addition to the tornadoes, there were other reports of downburst wind damage throughout central and southern Minnesota. ||Over a two week period, there were numerous road trips to determine if tornadoes occurred in rural areas. Due to the several storm spotters, emergency managers, and those who had drones to further the investigation, plus individuals recording the brief touchdowns, there were 16 tornadoes that occurred in Chanhassen NWS forecast area. Most of the tornadoes were confined to rural farmland, but there were a few that did damage farms, sheds, and outbuildings.

A small complex of storms that moved out of northeast South Dakota late Saturday morning caused a concentrated area of straight-line wind damage. The Canby, Minnesota airport measured a wind gust of 72 mph. These type of wind speeds continued across portions of Yellow Medicine and Lac Qui Parle counties where hundreds of trees and power lines were blown down. There was another wind gust of 68 mph at the Department of Transportation road sensor (Near Hanley Falls, MN) that went offline as the gust was recorded. ||The worst area occurred in the city of Granite Falls where winds tore open two roofs and allowed water to pour through ventilation ducts into classrooms and hallways, causing hundreds of thousands of dollars in damage to the Yellow Medicine East School District building. The Granite Fun restaurant and golf course also sustained damage with 55 to 60 trees blown down. Due to the extensive damage, the city of Granite Falls declared a state of emergency. ||Although wind damage occurred east of Granite Falls, mainly between Sacred Heart to Renville, this storm weakened as it moved eastward toward the western part of the Twin Cities.||A secondary area of storms developed across south central Minnesota during the mid-late afternoon near New Ulm, and produced another area of significant wind damage eastward toward Cannon Falls, and Red Wing. There was a swath of tree damage around Cannon Falls where one house lost its roof, with other having minor roof damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A measured wind report from the Department of Transportation wind sensor.

These type of wind speeds continued across portions of Yellow Medicine and Lac Qui Parle counties where hundreds of trees and power lines were blown down. There was another wind gust of 68 mph at the Department of Transportation road sensor (Near Hanley Falls, MN) that went offline as the gust was recorded. ||The worst area occurred in the city of Granite Falls where winds tore open two roofs and allowed water to pour through ventilation ducts into classrooms and hallways, causing hundreds of thousands of dollars in damage to the Yellow Medicine East School District building. The Granite Fun restaurant and golf course also sustained damage with 55 to 60 trees blown down. Due to the extensive damage, the city of Granite Falls declared a state of emergency. ||Although wind damage occurred east of Granite Falls, mainly between Sacred Heart to Renville, this storm weakened as it moved eastward toward the western part of the Twin Cities.||A secondary area of storms developed across south central Minnesota during the mid-late afternoon near New Ulm, and produced another area of significant wind damage eastward toward Cannon Falls, and Red Wing. There was a swath of tree damage around Cannon Falls where one house lost its roof, with other having minor roof damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread wind damage occurred around the city of Granite Falls. Numerous trees, power lines and structural damage occurred in the city with the Yellow Medicine East School District main building sustaining major damage. The winds tore off the heating and ventilation systems on the roofs, opening the duct work to the rain. Water poured through the ventilation system and directly into classrooms, hallways and on equipment.  On the south side of the Minnesota River, the Granite Run golf course suffered significant damage to trees and their local restaurant.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of storms that originated over southern North Dakota Sunday evening, August 23rd, moved over west-central Minnesota during the early morning and caused significant wind gusts, including at the Benson Airport. Some damage was noted around Benson as 84 MPH winds gusted at the airport.

A complex of storms that originated over southern North Dakota Sunday evening, August 23rd, moved over west-central Minnesota during the early morning and caused significant wind gusts, including at the Benson Airport. Some damage was noted around Benson as 84 MPH winds gusted at the airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A farm irrigator was tipped over and a grain bin caved in during the early morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon of Friday, August, 14th, thunderstorms developed along a warm front across central Minnesota, with additional development southward in the warm sector by the late afternoon, and early evening. These storms were able to produce many tornadoes, most of which were rated EF-0, but three were rated EF-1. In addition to the tornadoes, there were other reports of downburst wind damage throughout central and southern Minnesota. ||Over a two week period, there were numerous road trips to determine if tornadoes occurred in rural areas. Due to the several storm spotters, emergency managers, and those who had drones to further the investigation, plus individuals recording the brief touchdowns, there were 16 tornadoes that occurred in Chanhassen NWS forecast area. Most of the tornadoes were confined to rural farmland, but there were a few that did damage farms, sheds, and outbuildings.

Due to the several storm spotters, emergency managers, and those who had drones to further the investigation, plus individuals recording the brief touchdowns, there were 16 tornadoes that occurred in Chanhassen NWS forecast area. Most of the tornadoes were confined to rural farmland, but there were a few that did damage farms, sheds, and outbuildings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS survey found damage consistent with an EF-1 tornado in northeastern Todd County about 6 miles west of Cushing. The tornado touched down 0.2 miles northwest of Big Lake and traveled north-northeast for nearly 5.2 miles as it moved into Morrison County, near Lincoln. The most significant damage in Todd County was along Star Lake, where several hundred trees were broken and uprooted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of storms moved across north central and northeastern Minnesota during the afternoon and evening hours of the 7th. Heavy rainfall combined with the slow movement of the storms lead to some minor flooding in spots.

Due to the extensive damage, the city of Granite Falls declared a state of emergency. ||Although wind damage occurred east of Granite Falls, mainly between Sacred Heart to Renville, this storm weakened as it moved eastward toward the western part of the Twin Cities.||A secondary area of storms developed across south central Minnesota during the mid-late afternoon near New Ulm, and produced another area of significant wind damage eastward toward Cannon Falls, and Red Wing. There was a swath of tree damage around Cannon Falls where one house lost its roof, with other having minor roof damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few large trees were blown down between Sacred Heart, and Renville, including a large tree that fell on a house in Renville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon of Friday, August, 14th, thunderstorms developed along a warm front across central Minnesota, with additional development southward in the warm sector by the late afternoon, and early evening. These storms were able to produce many tornadoes, most of which were rated EF-0, but three were rated EF-1. In addition to the tornadoes, there were other reports of downburst wind damage throughout central and southern Minnesota. ||Over a two week period, there were numerous road trips to determine if tornadoes occurred in rural areas. Due to the several storm spotters, emergency managers, and those who had drones to further the investigation, plus individuals recording the brief touchdowns, there were 16 tornadoes that occurred in Chanhassen NWS forecast area. Most of the tornadoes were confined to rural farmland, but there were a few that did damage farms, sheds, and outbuildings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A measured wind report from a private weather sensor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approached the International Border region from the north the afternoon of the 19th. Storms developed ahead of the front north of the border and showed a weakening trend as they crossed into far northeastern Minnesota. One of the storms was able to sustain itself briefly as it passed over Crane Lake and a few reports of severe hail were received.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening of Sunday, August 9th, a storm developed in Carver County, west of the NWS office. Several more storms developed and trained over the same areas that affected eastern Carver, far northern Scott, and southern Hennepin Counties for an hour. The storms were difficult to recognize the severity at first as they developed nearly overhead from the radar location in Chanhassen.  ||Once the storms weakened over southern Hennepin County, more storms reformed northwest of the Twin Cities, and again produced very large hail around Buffalo, Montrose, Hanover, Rockford, and Loretto.  These storms propagated east-southeast toward the western suburbs of the Twin Cities and produced damaging hail between Lake Minnetonka, Eden Prairie and Edina. Slowly, the storms began to propagate to the southeast across south-central Minnesota, by the early morning. ||There was a period where hailstones were as large as baseballs (based on pictures from social media) across the western metro area. The two areas that had pictures of the largest stones were Loretto and Victoria.||Based on social media pictures, radar, and MRMS information, there were four areas that likely had hail up to baseball size hail, or possibly greater. The first area was the southern part of Kanabec County. The second area was from southeast Wright County, eastward across Hennepin between Lake Minnetonka to Edina. The third area was near Jordan, southeast to Lydia in Scott County. The fourth area was along the Dakota, Rice County line near Northfield.||Another unique part of these storms is that the hail was nearly continuous for 5 to 15 minutes, and some of the larger hailstones caused significant damage to cars and roofs.||In addition to all of the hail reports, several lines of thunderstorms that continued over the same areas, produced a wide area of very heavy rainfall reports with the heaviest at the NWS office at 5.53 inches. Reports between two and four inches that weren't included in the event were concentrated between Victoria, Chanhassen, Shakopee, Eden Prairie, Bloomington and Lake Minnetonka.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous sources from social media pictures, to trained spotters and official NWS observations, reported and measured very large hail for nearly 40 minutes from Victoria, eastward to the Carver, Hennepin County line. This includes some of the smaller communities between the two towns. ||The largest hailstone was measured at 2.75 inches in diameter and fell near Victoria. Two inch hail was measured at the NWS office. Due to the large hail size and almost a continuous line of storms redeveloping in the same areas, many roofs and cars were damaged.

The fourth area was along the Dakota, Rice County line near Northfield.||Another unique part of these storms is that the hail was nearly continuous for 5 to 15 minutes, and some of the larger hailstones caused significant damage to cars and roofs.||In addition to all of the hail reports, several lines of thunderstorms that continued over the same areas, produced a wide area of very heavy rainfall reports with the heaviest at the NWS office at 5.53 inches. Reports between two and four inches that weren't included in the event were concentrated between Victoria, Chanhassen, Shakopee, Eden Prairie, Bloomington and Lake Minnetonka.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe hail slowly decreased in size as it moved across Bloomington, with the largest measured hail size of 1.25 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening of Sunday, August 9th, a storm developed in Carver County, west of the NWS office. Several more storms developed and trained over the same areas that affected eastern Carver, far northern Scott, and southern Hennepin Counties for an hour. The storms were difficult to recognize the severity at first as they developed nearly overhead from the radar location in Chanhassen.  ||Once the storms weakened over southern Hennepin County, more storms reformed northwest of the Twin Cities, and again produced very large hail around Buffalo, Montrose, Hanover, Rockford, and Loretto.  These storms propagated east-southeast toward the western suburbs of the Twin Cities and produced damaging hail between Lake Minnetonka, Eden Prairie and Edina. Slowly, the storms began to propagate to the southeast across south-central Minnesota, by the early morning. ||There was a period where hailstones were as large as baseballs (based on pictures from social media) across the western metro area. The two areas that had pictures of the largest stones were Loretto and Victoria.||Based on social media pictures, radar, and MRMS information, there were four areas that likely had hail up to baseball size hail, or possibly greater. The first area was the southern part of Kanabec County. The second area was from southeast Wright County, eastward across Hennepin between Lake Minnetonka to Edina. The third area was near Jordan, southeast to Lydia in Scott County. The fourth area was along the Dakota, Rice County line near Northfield.||Another unique part of these storms is that the hail was nearly continuous for 5 to 15 minutes, and some of the larger hailstones caused significant damage to cars and roofs.||In addition to all of the hail reports, several lines of thunderstorms that continued over the same areas, produced a wide area of very heavy rainfall reports with the heaviest at the NWS office at 5.53 inches. Reports between two and four inches that weren't included in the event were concentrated between Victoria, Chanhassen, Shakopee, Eden Prairie, Bloomington and Lake Minnetonka.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One report of quarter size hail was observed near St. Michaels. However, based on radar and similar reports earlier in the evening, there were likely larger hailstones west of St. Michaels, near Pelican Lake, and eastward through the town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms moved across portions of southeast Minnesota during the early morning of August 10th. These storms dropped hail from Dodge County east to Winona County. Most of the hail was the size of a quarter or smaller, except for some two inch diameter hail reported in Ridgeway (Winona County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail was reported near Mantorville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front pushed across Minnesota the evening of the 14th and led to the development of a line of storms ahead of the front. As the storms pushed across northeastern Minnesota, they produced all modes of severe weather. Heavy rainfall led to areas of flash flooding, mainly along the western Iron Range. The bulk of the severe reports consisted of damaging wind gusts that brought down numerous trees, leading to damage at homes and other buildings. Additionally, a few brief, weak tornadoes were embedded in a section of the line that surged out over Aitkin and Carlton counties. These tornadoes produced further damage to trees and properties, mainly embedded within larger areas of wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports of flooded roadways were received in Hibbing along US 169 along with many city streets as well.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were cleared by MnDOT along Highway 36 that had been downed by the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approached the International Border region from the north the afternoon of the 19th. Storms developed ahead of the front north of the border and showed a weakening trend as they crossed into far northeastern Minnesota. One of the storms was able to sustain itself briefly as it passed over Crane Lake and a few reports of severe hail were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime to quarter size hail fell for approximately 15 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the very early morning hours of Friday, August 14th, sporadic slow moving severe thunderstorms formed across portions of west central Minnesota. These resulted in reports of large hail, strong wind gusts, and flash flooding. Prior to sunrise and through Friday morning, additional storms developed back westward into southeast North Dakota, and they continued to fire across west central Minnesota. These storms continued to produce large hail, gusty winds, and flash flooding. By mid-afternoon, an area of surface low pressure tracked through west central Minnesota, dragging a cold front behind it. Five tornadoes occurred during this period across Grant County, Minnesota.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front swept across eastern North Dakota and the northwestern quarter of Minnesota during the evening of September 2nd. Around 8 pm CDT, a line of thunderstorms stretched from central Walsh County (ND) southwestward into eastern Eddy County (ND). These storms progressed nearly straight east, so that by 10:30 pm CDT, they stretched from Waskish to Bemidji to just east of Detroit Lakes (MN). These storms produced strong wind gusts at numerous locations along this track.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree branches were blown down in Stauss Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unusual warmth with a week of temperatures mainly in the 70s (even 50s and 60s on November 9 less than a day before this storm) was brought to an abrupt end as a deep trough of low pressure moved out of Rockies and into the Plains. An intense lower- to mid-level frontal zone oozed eastward as a strong jet lifted out across the Plains on November 10, initiating widespread precipitation. More rapid shallow cooling occurred across northwest Iowa and northeast Nebraska, and led to a swath of freezing rain which accumulated from a tenth to a half inch during the overnight and morning hours, before changing to snow in the afternoon. Heavier and more persistent snowfall occurred with the deeper cooling to the northwest across southeast South Dakota, far northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota, where amounts ranged from 3 to 7 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 4.5 inches at Edgerton and Pipestone.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening of Saturday, September 5th, elevated thunderstorms developed over west-central and southwest Minnesota along the nose of a strengthening low level jet. Steep lapse rates of 8-9 C/km and bulk shear values of 50-70 knots were ample enough to support rotating storms and strong updrafts. Thus, storms were capable of producing very large hail. The largest hail stone measured was 2 inches in diameter over southwest Minnesota near Sacred Heart. There were also numerous quarter to golf ball size hail stones that fell across southern Minnesota late Saturday evening, through early Sunday morning. Some of the storms transitioned to damaging winds over south central Minnesota where a few wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph occurred and caused damage.

Unusual warmth with a week of temperatures mainly in the 70s (even 50s and 60s on November 9 less than a day before this storm) was brought to an abrupt end as a deep trough of low pressure moved out of Rockies and into the Plains. An intense lower- to mid-level frontal zone oozed eastward as a strong jet lifted out across the Plains on November 10, initiating widespread precipitation. More rapid shallow cooling occurred across northwest Iowa and northeast Nebraska, and led to a swath of freezing rain which accumulated from a tenth to a half inch during the overnight and morning hours, before changing to snow in the afternoon. Heavier and more persistent snowfall occurred with the deeper cooling to the northwest across southeast South Dakota, far northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota, where amounts ranged from 3 to 7 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall, heavy at times between 1300-1700CST, totaled 7.2 inches at Hills.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated thunderstorm brought severe winds near Graceville.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated sixty mph wind took the top off an evergreen tree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late afternoon and evening of Friday, August 7th, a warm front lifted through the northwest quarter of Minnesota. Thunderstorms fired up, and produced large hail, damaging wind gusts, and a few tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wall cloud produced a tornado with multiple brief touchdowns west of Fishhook Lake and near highway 71. Several trees were damaged. Peak winds were estimated at 70 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong, late season cold front worked through the Northland on the evening of the 25th. Ample shear was in place along with cold air aloft to lead to the development of storms ahead of the front that were efficient small hail producers. One storm did produce hail to the size of nickels as it passed near Grand Rapids.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel-sized hail was covering the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms moved across portions of southeast Minnesota during the early morning of August 10th. These storms dropped hail from Dodge County east to Winona County. Most of the hail was the size of a quarter or smaller, except for some two inch diameter hail reported in Ridgeway (Winona County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two inch diameter hail was reported near Ridgeway.

A small complex of storms that moved out of northeast South Dakota late Saturday morning caused a concentrated area of straight-line wind damage. The Canby, Minnesota airport measured a wind gust of 72 mph. These type of wind speeds continued across portions of Yellow Medicine and Lac Qui Parle counties where hundreds of trees and power lines were blown down. There was another wind gust of 68 mph at the Department of Transportation road sensor (Near Hanley Falls, MN) that went offline as the gust was recorded. ||The worst area occurred in the city of Granite Falls where winds tore open two roofs and allowed water to pour through ventilation ducts into classrooms and hallways, causing hundreds of thousands of dollars in damage to the Yellow Medicine East School District building. The Granite Fun restaurant and golf course also sustained damage with 55 to 60 trees blown down. Due to the extensive damage, the city of Granite Falls declared a state of emergency. ||Although wind damage occurred east of Granite Falls, mainly between Sacred Heart to Renville, this storm weakened as it moved eastward toward the western part of the Twin Cities.||A secondary area of storms developed across south central Minnesota during the mid-late afternoon near New Ulm, and produced another area of significant wind damage eastward toward Cannon Falls, and Red Wing. There was a swath of tree damage around Cannon Falls where one house lost its roof, with other having minor roof damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning bolt struck a park shelter where 40 people were attending a birthday party. Three individuals were injured, but with non-life-threatening injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated showers developed across portions of southeast Minnesota during the late afternoon of August 22nd. Funnel clouds associated with these showers were sighted near Kasson (Dodge County), Dodge Center (Dodge County) and Rochester (Olmsted County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was sighted east of Kasson.

Unusual warmth with a week of temperatures mainly in the 70s (even 50s and 60s on November 9 less than a day before this storm) was brought to an abrupt end as a deep trough of low pressure moved out of Rockies and into the Plains. An intense lower- to mid-level frontal zone oozed eastward as a strong jet lifted out across the Plains on November 10, initiating widespread precipitation. More rapid shallow cooling occurred across northwest Iowa and northeast Nebraska, and led to a swath of freezing rain which accumulated from a tenth to a half inch during the overnight and morning hours, before changing to snow in the afternoon. Heavier and more persistent snowfall occurred with the deeper cooling to the northwest across southeast South Dakota, far northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota, where amounts ranged from 3 to 7 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wintry mix produced less than a tenth of an inch of ice accumulation before changing to snow midday.  Snowfall totaled 4.1 inches 2 miles northeast of Lakefield.
