EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 5.0 inches at Lake Wilson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed into a volatile airmass across northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota by late afternoon with up to 3000 J/kg MLCAPE. Scattered thunderstorms erupted with stronger and rotating storms producing small hail and a tornado in southwest Minnesota near Worthington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief touchdown occurred in a corn field, with an observed twisting to the crops.  The tornado travelled a very short distance to the northeast and lifted as it moved near a small hog house. The hog house was slightly displaced from its foundation.  Property damage costs are rough estimates.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late morning of Saturday, July 11th, several clusters of thunderstorms developed across southwest, and west central Minnesota, and moved southeast along a strengthening gradient of instability and wind shear. There were numerous reports of large hail as a complex of storms began to organize, with hail stones measured up to 2.5 inches in diameter from a swath across southern Kandiyohi, western Renville, southwest McLeod, and parts of northwest, and central Sibley County. ||This hail storm was very significant in terms of crop damage as the swath of hail was up to 60 miles long and six miles wide. This affected thousands of acres of corn, soy beans, kidney beans, sugar beets, peas, sweet corn, and alfalfa. Acres upon acres were impacted and millions of dollars were lost. The University of Minnesota Extension looked into the damage and indicated the extent of damage to corn stalk and soybean farms was critical due to the time of year. The worst areas were in Renville, McLeod and Sibley Counties of south central Minnesota.||The storm shifted from a large hail event, to a wind event as a bow echo developed across Nicollet County and moved southeast quickly over portions of south central Minnesota. The most intense part of the storm early was northwest of Mankato, where a tornado touched down and uprooted trees and tore part of a roof off a machine shed. The bow echo continued to produce wind damage southeast across Blue Earth, and portions of Le Sueur Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening of Friday, July 17th, a line of strong to severe thunderstorms that developed across northwest Minnesota, propagated south-southeast across central Minnesota and became a well defined bow echo segment. There were two areas that had the worst damage. One occurred in Stevens County where a swath of wind damage extended 3/4 of a mile to the southeast of Chokio, and about 1/4 of a mile wide. Many trees were snapped off just above the base, while others were uprooted. The second area was associated with straight-line winds, occasional downbursts, and two tornadoes that hit northern Isanti, and northern Chisago Counties. ||There was ample instability and wind shear during the evening to hold supercell characteristics along this bow echo segment. As the storm bowed out to the southeast late in the evening, less instability and wind shear diminished the organized nature of the bow segment. However, a one bow echo segment allowed for two tornadic storms near Day and Rush City.  ||There was continued weakening of this bow echo overnight, with more individual line segments which caused brief, and isolated wind damage. Even local airport wind sensors, and private wind sensors were measuring wind gusts in the 40 to 50 mph range past midnight LST. The exception was in the Twin Cities metro area where the Flying Cloud airport measured a wind gust of 59 mph, and the Minneapolis/St. Paul airport measured a wind gust of 55 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were blown down in Rush City, along Highway 61.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Outflow from a large convective complex in northeast South Dakota and west central Minnesota surged into the Highway 14 corridor, with severe winds preceding the actual storms by up to a half hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An 11 inch diameter tree limb fell on a car, causing damage to the vehicle.  Property damage costs are rough estimates.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of thunderstorms developed over west central Minnesota during the afternoon of Wednesday, July 8th along a cold front. These storms quickly became severe just northwest of Douglas County where a tornado occurred. A few of the storms produced minor wind damage near Hewitt and Bertha, along with quarter size hail near the Todd and Cass County border. The storms moved into east central Minnesota and continued to produce some large hail and damaging winds. A secondary area of storms developed early Thursday morning across southern Minnesota, and moved east-northeast across south central, and into east central Minnesota, including the Twin Cities metro area. Several large hail stones occurred with these storms; one of the largest, measuring 1.25 inches in diameter, occurred near Hastings.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A local trained spotter reported dime to occasional quarter size hail in the town of Belle Plaine. Radar indicated that the strongest part of the storm occurred southwest of Belle Plaine which likely had hail stones much larger. There were also strong winds that caused some large branches to be blown down, and it also moved outdoor furniture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of thunderstorms developed over west central Minnesota during the afternoon of Wednesday, July 8th along a cold front. These storms quickly became severe just northwest of Douglas County where a tornado occurred. A few of the storms produced minor wind damage near Hewitt and Bertha, along with quarter size hail near the Todd and Cass County border. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A swath of large hail, up to quarter size, fell on the south side of Hennepin County around Bloomington, to Richfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late morning of Saturday, July 11th, several clusters of thunderstorms developed across southwest, and west central Minnesota, and moved southeast along a strengthening gradient of instability and wind shear. The most intense part of the storm early was northwest of Mankato, where a tornado touched down and uprooted trees and tore part of a roof off a machine shed. The bow echo continued to produce wind damage southeast across Blue Earth, and portions of Le Sueur Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The swath of large hail, up to 2.5 inches in diameter, and the continued wind driven hail, occurred in the northern part of Sibley County, southwest and south of Stewart. Major crop damage continued along this path.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across east central South Dakota and southwest Minnesota on the outflow from more organized storms to the northeast by late evening on September 5.  With around 55 knots of deep-layer shear and elevated instability to around 2000 J/kg, a few storms became supercellular with large hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level disturbance moving from Wyoming into South Dakota initiated late evening convection in an environment with around 2000 J/kg MLCAPE and deep-layer shear of around 50 knots. Storms expanded and rapidly evolved into a large-scale bow echo.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minnesota RWIS station MN030, 3 miles south southwest of Hendricks, measured a peak wind gust of 62 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very anomalous moist atmosphere was in place for this flash flood event to develop. Precipitable water values (PWATs) were over 2 inches across southern Minnesota which is well over climatological normals. The 2.25 inches of precipitable water tallied from the 7 PM weather balloon launch at Chanhassen was just below record record levels for the day and was above the 95th percentile for this time of year. ||There were very subtle outflow boundaries during the afternoon from earlier thunderstorms in southern Minnesota. There was enough surface boundary interaction to develop a few funnel clouds where the thunderstorms formed. There were even two tornado touchdowns near Gaylord and a funnel cloud near Lafayette. ||The main event was the flash flood event where storms formed Saturday afternoon between Redwood Falls, and the Twin Cities metro area. There were several waves of thunderstorms Saturday evening that propagated east-southeast across far southern Minnesota. The first area was concentrated between Redwood Falls and Henderson, Minnesota. This areas held together for nearly 4 hours before shifting slightly to the southeast toward the Minnesota River. Between 9 PM and 1 AM LST, the heaviest precipitation axis shifted along the Minnesota River Valley from Granite Falls, southeast to New Ulm, Mankato and then toward Waseca. After 1 AM LST, a much broader area of showers and thunderstorms started to develop across the rest of southern and western Minnesota. However, there still remained several hours of moderate to heavy rainfall in the same areas that received the heavier rainfall Saturday evening.||Based on MRMS-FLASH (Multi-Radar Multi-Sensor) (Flooded Locations and Simulated Hydrographs), excessive rainfall rates, and training over the same locations for a long period of time, the first concentrated area of flash flooding occurred over Sibley County where the recurrence interval was over 200 years for the 3, 6, and 12 hour periods. By midnight, this area of 200 year recurrence interval moved westward into southeastern Renville County. Between midnight and 4 AM LST, this area of 200 year recurrence interval moved east-southeast and oriented from Redwood Falls, southeast along the Minnesota River Valley, east/southeast toward Waseca. ||The heaviest rainfall and the hardest hit areas in terms of rainfall totals were over southeast Renville, southern Sibley, Nicollet, southwest Le Sueur, northern Blue Earth, and a part of northwest Waseca Counties.||The highest known total for this storm came from a trained observer near Winthrop in Sibley County where they measured 11.50 inches. Other high totals in that area included 10.70 inches near Lafayette and 9.15 inches in Gibbon. ||The state climate office categorized the heavy rainfall event in south central Minnesota  as a mega-rain.  Their criteria for a mega-rain is six inches of rain or more over an area of roughly 1000 square miles.  This was the first mega-rain event in Minnesota since 2016.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A measured rainfall report over the last 24-hours at Waterville was 7.33 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved southeast across eastern Minnesota, and western Wisconsin during the late afternoon, and evening of Saturday, July 18th. Along this front, a line of severe thunderstorms develop which initially was producing large hail, but became tornadic during the evening in parts of the eastern Twin Cities metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS Storm Survey indicated that an EF1 tornado occurred in far southern Washington County, in a rural area, north of Hastings, Minnesota.  ||The tornado spun up 5 miles north of Hastings, Minnesota, and tracked eastward for 3.2 miles until it moved across the St. Croix River, and into Wisconsin.  Extensive tree damage occurred along its path, with the heaviest damage on top of the bluff as it moved across the St. Croix River into Wisconsin. A few farm outbuildings were also damaged. The tornado had a maximum speed of 100 mph, with a maximum width of 400 yards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a weak mid-level wave moved from Wyoming to western Minnesota, the southern edge of an isolated elevated thunderstorm became severe, and produced isolated early afternoon wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind estimate through social media report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late morning of Saturday, July 11th, several clusters of thunderstorms developed across southwest, and west central Minnesota, and moved southeast along a strengthening gradient of instability and wind shear. There were numerous reports of large hail as a complex of storms began to organize, with hail stones measured up to 2.5 inches in diameter from a swath across southern Kandiyohi, western Renville, southwest McLeod, and parts of northwest, and central Sibley County. ||This hail storm was very significant in terms of crop damage as the swath of hail was up to 60 miles long and six miles wide. This affected thousands of acres of corn, soy beans, kidney beans, sugar beets, peas, sweet corn, and alfalfa. Acres upon acres were impacted and millions of dollars were lost. The University of Minnesota Extension looked into the damage and indicated the extent of damage to corn stalk and soybean farms was critical due to the time of year. The worst areas were in Renville, McLeod and Sibley Counties of south central Minnesota.||The storm shifted from a large hail event, to a wind event as a bow echo developed across Nicollet County and moved southeast quickly over portions of south central Minnesota. The most intense part of the storm early was northwest of Mankato, where a tornado touched down and uprooted trees and tore part of a roof off a machine shed. The bow echo continued to produce wind damage southeast across Blue Earth, and portions of Le Sueur Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: About one dozen large branches were broken.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level wave dropped southeast from the Red River Valley through southwest Minnesota by midday, igniting convection along a southeastward pushing boundary.  With unidirectional deep-layer shear around 30 knots and MLCAPE approaching 1500 J/kg, a few supercells developed with very large hail.

An upper-level wave dropped southeast from the Red River Valley through southwest Minnesota by midday, igniting convection along a southeastward pushing boundary.  With unidirectional deep-layer shear around 30 knots and MLCAPE approaching 1500 J/kg, a few supercells developed with very large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wall cloud produced a brief funnel cloud.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level wave dropped southeast from the Red River Valley through southwest Minnesota by midday, igniting convection along a southeastward pushing boundary.  With unidirectional deep-layer shear around 30 knots and MLCAPE approaching 1500 J/kg, a few supercells developed with very large hail.

An elevated mixed layer and strong surface heating enhanced MLCAPE to 2500-3500 J/kg, with a DCAPE reservoir of up to 1500 J/kg just ahead of an advancing cold front. Storms remained most organized west of the James River in the early evening, where deep-layer shear was maximized at 30-40 knots. After weakening in eastern South Dakota, an isolated severe storm redeveloped in southwest Minnesota with large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Most of the hail was nickel sized, but a few stones were as large as quarters. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed into a volatile airmass across northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota by late afternoon with up to 3000 J/kg MLCAPE. Scattered thunderstorms erupted with stronger and rotating storms producing small hail and a tornado in southwest Minnesota near Worthington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel was reported near mile marker 31 on Interstate 90.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening of Friday, July 17th, a line of strong to severe thunderstorms that developed across northwest Minnesota, propagated south-southeast across central Minnesota and became a well defined bow echo segment. There were two areas that had the worst damage. One occurred in Stevens County where a swath of wind damage extended 3/4 of a mile to the southeast of Chokio, and about 1/4 of a mile wide. Many trees were snapped off just above the base, while others were uprooted. The second area was associated with straight-line winds, occasional downbursts, and two tornadoes that hit northern Isanti, and northern Chisago Counties. ||There was ample instability and wind shear during the evening to hold supercell characteristics along this bow echo segment. As the storm bowed out to the southeast late in the evening, less instability and wind shear diminished the organized nature of the bow segment. However, a one bow echo segment allowed for two tornadic storms near Day and Rush City.  ||There was continued weakening of this bow echo overnight, with more individual line segments which caused brief, and isolated wind damage. Even local airport wind sensors, and private wind sensors were measuring wind gusts in the 40 to 50 mph range past midnight LST. The exception was in the Twin Cities metro area where the Flying Cloud airport measured a wind gust of 59 mph, and the Minneapolis/St. Paul airport measured a wind gust of 55 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado made contact just west of Day and traveled just north of a few houses, then hit a farm where multiples trees were uprooted or broken. A partially filled grain bin was also blown off its foundation. The tornado continued across open fields and wooded areas before dissipating. An anemometer on the southern edge of the tornado measured 87 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gusts accompanied hail up to golf ball size.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms developed across northwest Minnesota during the afternoon of Monday, July 13th. These storms developed and moved southeast toward central Minnesota during the late evening and produced large hail in Morrison County. ||Although there were no reports of large hail across Todd County from this same storm that produced half dollar size hail in Morrison County, there was a band, based on MRMS hail algorithm, from west of Browerville to east of Browerville earlier that likely produced half dollar size hail or larger.

A large area of showers and thunderstorms developed across southwest Minnesota during the early morning hours of Tuesday, July 14th. These storms moved northeast across southern Minnesota during the morning. One of the storms became severe in Dakota County and produced golf ball size hail east of Vermillion. Although hail wasn't reported southwest of Vermillion, based on radar, severe hail likely fell between Empire and Hampton before moving east of Vermillion where golf ball size hail fell.

During the late morning of Saturday, July 11th, several clusters of thunderstorms developed across southwest, and west central Minnesota, and moved southeast along a strengthening gradient of instability and wind shear. There were numerous reports of large hail as a complex of storms began to organize, with hail stones measured up to 2.5 inches in diameter from a swath across southern Kandiyohi, western Renville, southwest McLeod, and parts of northwest, and central Sibley County. ||This hail storm was very significant in terms of crop damage as the swath of hail was up to 60 miles long and six miles wide. This affected thousands of acres of corn, soy beans, kidney beans, sugar beets, peas, sweet corn, and alfalfa. Acres upon acres were impacted and millions of dollars were lost. The University of Minnesota Extension looked into the damage and indicated the extent of damage to corn stalk and soybean farms was critical due to the time of year. The worst areas were in Renville, McLeod and Sibley Counties of south central Minnesota.||The storm shifted from a large hail event, to a wind event as a bow echo developed across Nicollet County and moved southeast quickly over portions of south central Minnesota. The most intense part of the storm early was northwest of Mankato, where a tornado touched down and uprooted trees and tore part of a roof off a machine shed. The bow echo continued to produce wind damage southeast across Blue Earth, and portions of Le Sueur Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A power pole was blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening of Friday, July 17th, a line of strong to severe thunderstorms that developed across northwest Minnesota, propagated south-southeast across central Minnesota and became a well defined bow echo segment. There were two areas that had the worst damage. One occurred in Stevens County where a swath of wind damage extended 3/4 of a mile to the southeast of Chokio, and about 1/4 of a mile wide. Many trees were snapped off just above the base, while others were uprooted. The second area was associated with straight-line winds, occasional downbursts, and two tornadoes that hit northern Isanti, and northern Chisago Counties. ||There was ample instability and wind shear during the evening to hold supercell characteristics along this bow echo segment. As the storm bowed out to the southeast late in the evening, less instability and wind shear diminished the organized nature of the bow segment. However, a one bow echo segment allowed for two tornadic storms near Day and Rush City.  ||There was continued weakening of this bow echo overnight, with more individual line segments which caused brief, and isolated wind damage. Even local airport wind sensors, and private wind sensors were measuring wind gusts in the 40 to 50 mph range past midnight LST. The exception was in the Twin Cities metro area where the Flying Cloud airport measured a wind gust of 59 mph, and the Minneapolis/St. Paul airport measured a wind gust of 55 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was blown down in Jordan. Minor damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Localized rainfall of 2 to 3 inches on July 5-6 pushed the Rock River back into minor flood stage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: With rainfall of 2.09 inches at Edgerton and 2.04 inches at Luverne, the Rock River near Hardwick (gage site HRDM5) rose briefly into minor flood stage with a crest 0.27 feet above flood stage.  Low-lying agricultural lands near the river were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An elevated mixed layer and strong surface heating enhanced MLCAPE to 2500-3500 J/kg, with a DCAPE reservoir of up to 1500 J/kg just ahead of an advancing cold front. Storms remained most organized west of the James River in the early evening, where deep-layer shear was maximized at 30-40 knots. After weakening in eastern South Dakota, an isolated severe storm redeveloped in southwest Minnesota with large hail.

An upper-level wave dropped southeast from the Red River Valley through southwest Minnesota by midday, igniting convection along a southeastward pushing boundary.  With unidirectional deep-layer shear around 30 knots and MLCAPE approaching 1500 J/kg, a few supercells developed with very large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pair of supercell storms developed on the southern periphery of a bowing system across northeast South Dakota and west central Minnesota within an environment with around 2000 J/kg MLCAPE and 40 knots of deep-layer shear.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Half of a twenty year old apple tree was broken off by strong winds. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of thunderstorms developed over west central Minnesota during the afternoon of Wednesday, July 8th along a cold front. These storms quickly became severe just northwest of Douglas County where a tornado occurred. A few of the storms produced minor wind damage near Hewitt and Bertha, along with quarter size hail near the Todd and Cass County border. The storms moved into east central Minnesota and continued to produce some large hail and damaging winds. A secondary area of storms developed early Thursday morning across southern Minnesota, and moved east-northeast across south central, and into east central Minnesota, including the Twin Cities metro area. Several large hail stones occurred with these storms; one of the largest, measuring 1.25 inches in diameter, occurred near Hastings.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms that developed across North Dakota late Tuesday evening, moved quickly across west central, and central Minnesota during the morning of Wednesday, July 8th. Numerous trees were downed and several outbuildings and sheds were damaged. Based on pictures and drone video over the damage area east of Browerville, all of the damage was associated with downburst winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The local emergency manager, and pictures and drone video of damage, indicated that downburst winds caused damage to a few outbuildings and sheds northeast of Browerville, and a swath of large trees was blown down approximately 8 miles east of Browerville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm developed over the eastern Twin Cities Metro area during the afternoon of Sunday, July 5th. This storm was nearly stationary for an hour and produced up to 2 inches of rainfall in a short period of time. Four separate flood reports were noted with roads temporarily closed around Maplewood.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were several reports of roads flooded due to rainfall amounts of 1 to 3 inches in a very short period of time. These heavy rainfall rates, combined with the urban area (mostly runoff from the streets), led to several flooded roads and a couple closures. Specifically, White Bear Avenue, Lakewood, and Maryland Streets were flooded at one point with stalled vehicles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a weak mid-level wave moved from Wyoming to western Minnesota, the southern edge of an isolated elevated thunderstorm became severe, and produced isolated early afternoon wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was toppled on a farmstead. Property damage costs are rough estimates. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure lifting northeastward brought a short but heavy period of snow to the region during the evening of the 10th before ending during the early morning hours. Snowfall reports of 6 to 10 inches were recorded from the Brainerd Lakes area into the Twin Ports and Minnesota Arrowhead with a total of 11 inches recorded near Motley. Models struggled with this system right up to the onset of the storm and the area of heaviest snow extended further west than anticipated. Much of the snow fell in about 6 hours and started during the evening commute in many locations. Some of the Brainerd area received just over 6 inches of snow in as little as 3 hours during this event.

During the evening of Friday, July 17th, a line of strong to severe thunderstorms that developed across northwest Minnesota, propagated south-southeast across central Minnesota and became a well defined bow echo segment. There were two areas that had the worst damage. One occurred in Stevens County where a swath of wind damage extended 3/4 of a mile to the southeast of Chokio, and about 1/4 of a mile wide. Many trees were snapped off just above the base, while others were uprooted. The second area was associated with straight-line winds, occasional downbursts, and two tornadoes that hit northern Isanti, and northern Chisago Counties. ||There was ample instability and wind shear during the evening to hold supercell characteristics along this bow echo segment. As the storm bowed out to the southeast late in the evening, less instability and wind shear diminished the organized nature of the bow segment. However, a one bow echo segment allowed for two tornadic storms near Day and Rush City.  ||There was continued weakening of this bow echo overnight, with more individual line segments which caused brief, and isolated wind damage. Even local airport wind sensors, and private wind sensors were measuring wind gusts in the 40 to 50 mph range past midnight LST. The exception was in the Twin Cities metro area where the Flying Cloud airport measured a wind gust of 59 mph, and the Minneapolis/St. Paul airport measured a wind gust of 55 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A NWS Storm Survey determined that severe straight-line winds and occasional downburst winds, damaged hundreds of trees between Harris and Rush City along County Road 30. Some of the trees landed on vehicles, roofs and sheds. One machine shed had its roof blown off.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the morning of Tuesday, November, 10th, a cold front had moved across Minnesota and western Wisconsin.  A 500 MB short wave that developed across the central Rockies was moving northeast across Nebraska with an intensifying 850 MB low developing across Iowa Tuesday morning. At the surface, low pressure was across northern Missouri. Initially, the 850 MB low moved northward across Iowa, and stronger warm air advection across eastern Minnesota and western Wisconsin kept the precipitation in the form of sleet, freezing rain, and some snow Tuesday morning. Farther to the west and deeper into the colder air, light snow fell but it was mostly flurries or occasionally 2-4 mile visibility in light snow.  ||A dry slot developed, and moved over eastern Minnesota, while the main storm system moved farther west. Once the upper low moved over southwest Minnesota, a broader area of precipitation started to develop over central Minnesota where the dendritic growth zone was maximized together with abundant moisture content and lift. Also during the later afternoon, as the short wave moved closer to Minnesota and under the influence of a developing jet couplet over the Upper Midwest, the storm system became more organized as noted with a beautiful comma-shaped upper low moving over Minnesota. This was also where several snow bands started to develop, especially over central Minnesota where the combination of the intensifying short wave and upper-level jet occurred. At the surface, the low continued to strengthen and move northeast across eastern Iowa, and into northern Wisconsin by late evening. ||Just along and north of the comma-head shaped storm system was where the bulk of the heavier snowfall occurred, and it eventually produced 6-8 inches in central Minnesota, northeast into the arrowhead region of Minnesota. Farther to the south and southeast, too much mixing and not enough heavy precipitation kept snowfall amounts under 6 inches. A general 2 to 4 inches fell northwest of Mankato, to Luck, Wisconsin. Isolated areas of 5 to 6 inches across the Twin Cities metro area, northeast toward St. Cloud and Mora. Over most of south-central and southeast Minnesota, snowfall amounts of 1 inch or less occurred. Some freezing rain did occur Tuesday morning, and into the afternoon, but only isolated areas received enough to cause problems.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trained weather observers and local airports reported light snow Tuesday afternoon. Heavier snowfall began during the late afternoon and evening before ending toward midnight. Snowfall amounts across the county averaged around 4 to 7 inches.

During the late morning of Saturday, July 11th, several clusters of thunderstorms developed across southwest, and west central Minnesota, and moved southeast along a strengthening gradient of instability and wind shear. There were numerous reports of large hail as a complex of storms began to organize, with hail stones measured up to 2.5 inches in diameter from a swath across southern Kandiyohi, western Renville, southwest McLeod, and parts of northwest, and central Sibley County. ||This hail storm was very significant in terms of crop damage as the swath of hail was up to 60 miles long and six miles wide. This affected thousands of acres of corn, soy beans, kidney beans, sugar beets, peas, sweet corn, and alfalfa. Acres upon acres were impacted and millions of dollars were lost. The University of Minnesota Extension looked into the damage and indicated the extent of damage to corn stalk and soybean farms was critical due to the time of year. The worst areas were in Renville, McLeod and Sibley Counties of south central Minnesota.||The storm shifted from a large hail event, to a wind event as a bow echo developed across Nicollet County and moved southeast quickly over portions of south central Minnesota. The most intense part of the storm early was northwest of Mankato, where a tornado touched down and uprooted trees and tore part of a roof off a machine shed. The bow echo continued to produce wind damage southeast across Blue Earth, and portions of Le Sueur Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a picture of quarter size hail on social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening of Friday, July 17th, a line of strong to severe thunderstorms that developed across northwest Minnesota, propagated south-southeast across central Minnesota and became a well defined bow echo segment. There were two areas that had the worst damage. One occurred in Stevens County where a swath of wind damage extended 3/4 of a mile to the southeast of Chokio, and about 1/4 of a mile wide. Many trees were snapped off just above the base, while others were uprooted. The second area was associated with straight-line winds, occasional downbursts, and two tornadoes that hit northern Isanti, and northern Chisago Counties. ||There was ample instability and wind shear during the evening to hold supercell characteristics along this bow echo segment. As the storm bowed out to the southeast late in the evening, less instability and wind shear diminished the organized nature of the bow segment. However, a one bow echo segment allowed for two tornadic storms near Day and Rush City.  ||There was continued weakening of this bow echo overnight, with more individual line segments which caused brief, and isolated wind damage. Even local airport wind sensors, and private wind sensors were measuring wind gusts in the 40 to 50 mph range past midnight LST. The exception was in the Twin Cities metro area where the Flying Cloud airport measured a wind gust of 59 mph, and the Minneapolis/St. Paul airport measured a wind gust of 55 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A measured wind report of 53 knots from a private weather station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms that developed over the Dakotas Tuesday evening moved eastward across western Minnesota after midnight. Gusty winds accompany the strongest storms as they moved out of the Dakotas with numerous measured surface observations of 40 to 50 mph. The highest measured wind speed near Donnelly occurred at Graceville where a Minnesota Department of Transportation wind sensor measured 42 mph. There was another wind gust of 53 mph at Big Stone RAWS station near the Minnesota/South Dakota border. A few stronger storms did develop in Stevens county where the wind damage occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm survey that was conducted by the local emergency manager, and storm chasers, concluded that a downburst caused wind damage from 4 miles southwest of Donnelly, to 2 miles northeast of Donnelly. The damage field was 1/2 mile wide and all the damage was to the east/northeast. All the damage outside of Donnelly was scattered trees blown down and some crop damage. There were multiple buildings in the town of Donnelly that had damage. Some siding and many shingles were blown off downtown structures. Some trees fell onto houses and buildings. Two metal buildings had their tin roofs partially removed and one had a steel garage door blown in.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the morning of Tuesday, November, 10th, a cold front had moved across Minnesota and western Wisconsin.  A 500 MB short wave that developed across the central Rockies was moving northeast across Nebraska with an intensifying 850 MB low developing across Iowa Tuesday morning. At the surface, low pressure was across northern Missouri. Initially, the 850 MB low moved northward across Iowa, and stronger warm air advection across eastern Minnesota and western Wisconsin kept the precipitation in the form of sleet, freezing rain, and some snow Tuesday morning. Farther to the west and deeper into the colder air, light snow fell but it was mostly flurries or occasionally 2-4 mile visibility in light snow.  ||A dry slot developed, and moved over eastern Minnesota, while the main storm system moved farther west. Once the upper low moved over southwest Minnesota, a broader area of precipitation started to develop over central Minnesota where the dendritic growth zone was maximized together with abundant moisture content and lift. Also during the later afternoon, as the short wave moved closer to Minnesota and under the influence of a developing jet couplet over the Upper Midwest, the storm system became more organized as noted with a beautiful comma-shaped upper low moving over Minnesota. This was also where several snow bands started to develop, especially over central Minnesota where the combination of the intensifying short wave and upper-level jet occurred. At the surface, the low continued to strengthen and move northeast across eastern Iowa, and into northern Wisconsin by late evening. ||Just along and north of the comma-head shaped storm system was where the bulk of the heavier snowfall occurred, and it eventually produced 6-8 inches in central Minnesota, northeast into the arrowhead region of Minnesota. Farther to the south and southeast, too much mixing and not enough heavy precipitation kept snowfall amounts under 6 inches. A general 2 to 4 inches fell northwest of Mankato, to Luck, Wisconsin. Isolated areas of 5 to 6 inches across the Twin Cities metro area, northeast toward St. Cloud and Mora. Over most of south-central and southeast Minnesota, snowfall amounts of 1 inch or less occurred. Some freezing rain did occur Tuesday morning, and into the afternoon, but only isolated areas received enough to cause problems.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain started in Freeborn County around 830 AM CST, and continued through the late morning before it ended. Some light precipitation occurred during the afternoon, and early evening but the bulk of the ice fell between 830 and 1130 AM and accumulated up to a quarter of an inch.

Also during the later afternoon, as the short wave moved closer to Minnesota and under the influence of a developing jet couplet over the Upper Midwest, the storm system became more organized as noted with a beautiful comma-shaped upper low moving over Minnesota. This was also where several snow bands started to develop, especially over central Minnesota where the combination of the intensifying short wave and upper-level jet occurred. At the surface, the low continued to strengthen and move northeast across eastern Iowa, and into northern Wisconsin by late evening. ||Just along and north of the comma-head shaped storm system was where the bulk of the heavier snowfall occurred, and it eventually produced 6-8 inches in central Minnesota, northeast into the arrowhead region of Minnesota. Farther to the south and southeast, too much mixing and not enough heavy precipitation kept snowfall amounts under 6 inches. A general 2 to 4 inches fell northwest of Mankato, to Luck, Wisconsin. Isolated areas of 5 to 6 inches across the Twin Cities metro area, northeast toward St. Cloud and Mora. Over most of south-central and southeast Minnesota, snowfall amounts of 1 inch or less occurred. Some freezing rain did occur Tuesday morning, and into the afternoon, but only isolated areas received enough to cause problems.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trained weather observers and local airports reported a mixture of sleet and light snow Tuesday afternoon. Heavier snowfall began during the late afternoon and evening before ending toward midnight. Snowfall amounts across the county averaged around 5 to 6 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure brought heavy snowfall and very strong winds to much of the Northland starting on Wednesday the 23rd and lingering into Thursday the 24th. This storm was unique in that widespread blizzard conditions occurred across the upper Midwest, including areas from the Brainerd Lakes east towards the North Shore, Twin Ports, and Interstate 35 corridor with many observed wind gusts between 50 and 70 mph. Very strong frontogenesis occurred along a trowal axis that extended across much of northern Wisconsin west into Minnesota. Warm air advection caused temperatures across most of east-central Minnesota to rise above freezing and produce mixed precipitation for several hours during the day. As the cold front gradually moved east, temperatures very quickly dropped and caused treacherous road conditions as mixed precipitation changed over to snow. Narrow bands of heavy snow set up over the Brainerd Lakes northeast towards the Iron Range during the early morning and early afternoon, and then translated east towards the Twin Ports, North Shore, and Interstate 35 corridor. The heavy snow bands coincided with the strong mid-level frontogenesis, which also produced very strong northwest winds that caused the widespread blizzard conditions. A maximum gust of 70 mph was recorded at the Bay of Grand Marais during the height of the storm. Damage was reported to buildings and other infrastructure, in addition to some power outages and widespread whiteout conditions that made travel difficult if not impossible.||As the cold front swept rapidly eastward, bitterly cold air moved into the region and led to the development of a topographically-forced gravity wave during the night of the 23rd that dropped several more inches of snow from Two Harbors to Superior to Holyoke. The eastern half of northeast Minnesota received 6 to 10 inches of snowfall with lesser amounts further west in addition to the widespread blizzard conditions.

A strong jet streak moved across Montana and North Dakota during the afternoon and early evening, providing enough lift to overcome a lid of warmer temperatures aloft across central South Dakota. Storms progressed east and southeast and utilized drying within the elevated layer to generate enough cooling to produce hail, and environmental deep-layer shear of 45 to 55 knots to organize into short bowing segments.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nine power poles were snapped off, with four tossed across 340th Avenue into the opposite ditch. Property damage costs are rough estimates.

A confluent trough developed across the Northern Plains and locked in the cold air which had pushed southward and produced numerous record low maximum and minimum temperatures. As an extended period of warm advection and frontogenesis intensified during the afternoon of October 24, a broad area of snowfall developed, with periods of moderate to briefly heavy snowfall during the morning and early afternoon of October 25. Numerous snowfall records were established on October 24th and 25th to go along with the record cold temperatures, which also propelled the month into the top 3 snowiest Octobers at Brookings, Flandreau, Marshall, Minneota, Tracy and Yankton (1st), Windom and De Smet (2nd), and Sibley, Sanborn and Pipestone (3rd).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 3.0 inches 8 miles southeast of Hendricks.

Broad warm advection developed early on December 29 as a strong trough aloft sheared northeast through the central and northern Plains. A period of strong frontogenesis shifted northeast, accompanied by a deep dendritic layer which briefly produced snowfall rates up to an inch per hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 3.3 inches 4 miles east of Hardwick.

As the cold front gradually moved east, temperatures very quickly dropped and caused treacherous road conditions as mixed precipitation changed over to snow. Narrow bands of heavy snow set up over the Brainerd Lakes northeast towards the Iron Range during the early morning and early afternoon, and then translated east towards the Twin Ports, North Shore, and Interstate 35 corridor. The heavy snow bands coincided with the strong mid-level frontogenesis, which also produced very strong northwest winds that caused the widespread blizzard conditions. A maximum gust of 70 mph was recorded at the Bay of Grand Marais during the height of the storm. Damage was reported to buildings and other infrastructure, in addition to some power outages and widespread whiteout conditions that made travel difficult if not impossible.||As the cold front swept rapidly eastward, bitterly cold air moved into the region and led to the development of a topographically-forced gravity wave during the night of the 23rd that dropped several more inches of snow from Two Harbors to Superior to Holyoke. The eastern half of northeast Minnesota received 6 to 10 inches of snowfall with lesser amounts further west in addition to the widespread blizzard conditions.

A strong jet streak moved across Montana and North Dakota during the afternoon and early evening, providing enough lift to overcome a lid of warmer temperatures aloft across central South Dakota. Storms progressed east and southeast and utilized drying within the elevated layer to generate enough cooling to produce hail, and environmental deep-layer shear of 45 to 55 knots to organize into short bowing segments.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large barn door was blown in and bent. Property damage costs are rough estimates.

The first of two rare October heavy snow events to the affect the region occurred on October 20th. An upper level low pressure trough coming in from the northwest riding over cold surface air brought heavy snow across west central Minnesota. The snowfall was very heavy at times enhanced by thunderstorms. Some snowfall amounts include, 6 inches at Browns Valley, 7 inches at Ortonville, and 8 inches at Beardsley.||These two rare heavy snow producing systems along with the abnormally cold air set some monthly snowfall and temperature records. Wheaton had its snowiest October on record along with its sixth coldest October on record.

Southeast Minnesota was hit with two rounds of severe storms during the afternoon and evening of June 2nd. The first round developed in the late afternoon and produced large hail that reached 2 inches in diameter in and around Winona and Pickwick (Winona County). The second round produced some sporadic wind damage. Three trees were blown down onto a house in Houston (Houston County), a semi was blown over north of Austin (Mower County) and an estimated 60 mph wind gust occurred southwest of Dover (Olmsted County). A fire at a Rochester (Olmsted County) apartment complex was started by a lightning strike.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick moving disturbance spread a swath of warm advection precipitation across South Dakota into southwest Minnesota during the early morning hours of October 20, 2020. While temperatures were initially warm enough to support rain, a combination of wet bulb cooling and the diabatic impact of intense lift in the mid-level frontogenetic zone caused a rapid and persistent change over to snow. A band of 3 to 7 inches of snowfall resulted from east central South Dakota near Interstate 29 eastward along the Buffalo Ridge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 3.0 inches at Westbrook, 2.5 inches 11 miles north of Storden and 2.3 inches at Windom.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For this event, the 500mb pattern featured ridging over eastern Minnesota and Wisconsin, with south-southwest flow over eastern North Dakota and the northwest quarter of Minnesota. At the surface, by the early afternoon of June 8th, a cold front had set up along a line from Cavalier to Oakes, North Dakota, with the Red River Valley into adjacent areas of the northwest quarter of Minnesota in the warm sector. In this warm sector, temperatures ranged in the 80s with dew points around 70. By the mid afternoon, thunderstorms initially formed along the boundary over northeast North Dakota and tracked into the far northwest corner of Minnesota. By late afternoon, additional storms formed around Cooperstown, North Dakota, and also tracked northeast into the far northwest corner of Minnesota. Kittson County, Minnesota, received several rounds of heavy rainfall. By mid evening, most storms tracked between Thief River Falls and Lake of the Woods. Finally, more storms formed and tracked across portions of west central Minnesota during the early morning hours of June 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado was viewed and recorded by at least two chase teams as it tracked intermittently for about 9 miles. The tornado snapped numerous trees in groves along its path, spreading debris up to 300 yards wide across one roadway. Peak winds were estimated at 100 mph.

An elderly lady was found dead on the southwest side of Rochester (Olmsted County) with the death likely caused by hypothermia. The lady wandered away from a senior living center during the early morning of October 26th and spent several hours outside when air temperatures were around 20 degrees with wind chills near 10. In addition, she was found in the snow that fell the previous day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An elderly lady wandered away from a senior living center on the southwest side of Rochester during the early morning of October 26th. She was later found in snow a few blocks away from the center after spending a few hours outdoors with air temperatures around 20 and wind chills near 10. The cause of death was likely hypothermia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early winter storm produced 6 to 9 inches across portions of central and southern Minnesota during the morning and late afternoon of Tuesday, October 20th. A weak surface trough moved across the Upper Midwest with modest warm air advection ahead of it. Initially, precipitation was light but increased by the late morning and afternoon as both higher than average Precipitable Water values were ingested into the system and a dual upper jet core was maximized across Minnesota. This allowed for a few hours of higher snowfall rates and led to isolated 8-9 inch totals where banding of these higher snowfall rates was located.  The snow set records in some locations for being so heavy so early in the season.||Some of the higher snowfall totals from this system include:||9.5 inches east of New Prague. |9.1 inches southwest of Apple Valley. |A swath of 8.5 to 9.0 inches between Prior Lake and Rosemount in Scott and Dakota Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts across the county ranged from 4 to 8 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The severe weather outbreak on Wednesday, June 17th, had a similar set up to the ones from June 7th and 8th. By the mid afternoon of June 17th, a stationary boundary was located over the central Dakotas, with eastern North Dakota and the adjacent area of the northwest quarter of Minnesota in the warm sector. Temperatures in the warm sector peaked in the upper 80s to middle 90s, with dew points in the 60s to around 70 degrees. The 500mb pattern featured ridging over the Great Lakes, with southwest flow over eastern North Dakota and northwest Minnesota. A lone thunderstorm initially formed over western Marshall County (Minnesota), and tracked northeast, passing west of Roseau. By that time, additional storms had formed between Jamestown and Grand Forks. This line of storms then tracked to the northeast, passing through the central and northern Red River Valley and Lake of the Woods region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Downburst winds blew across northern Barto, Eastern Soleer, and Mosse townships, snapping numerous trees in shelterbelts and farmsteads throughout the area. A steel clad pole shed was torn open and spread along a half mile path to the east, where a two stall shop was torn open.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The severe weather outbreak on Wednesday, June 17th, had a similar set up to the ones from June 7th and 8th. Temperatures in the warm sector peaked in the upper 80s to middle 90s, with dew points in the 60s to around 70 degrees. The 500mb pattern featured ridging over the Great Lakes, with southwest flow over eastern North Dakota and northwest Minnesota. A lone thunderstorm initially formed over western Marshall County (Minnesota), and tracked northeast, passing west of Roseau. By that time, additional storms had formed between Jamestown and Grand Forks. This line of storms then tracked to the northeast, passing through the central and northern Red River Valley and Lake of the Woods region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado tracked intermittently for about 2 miles. Several trees were snapped and numerous branches were broken down in shelterbelts and groves. Video and eyewitness reports described the tornado as being cut off by rear flank winds and rain as it tracked toward Greenbush. Peak winds were estimated at 95 mph.

A band of 3 to 7 inches of snowfall resulted from east central South Dakota near Interstate 29 eastward along the Buffalo Ridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The severe weather outbreak on Wednesday, June 17th, had a similar set up to the ones from June 7th and 8th. By the mid afternoon of June 17th, a stationary boundary was located over the central Dakotas, with eastern North Dakota and the adjacent area of the northwest quarter of Minnesota in the warm sector. Temperatures in the warm sector peaked in the upper 80s to middle 90s, with dew points in the 60s to around 70 degrees. The 500mb pattern featured ridging over the Great Lakes, with southwest flow over eastern North Dakota and northwest Minnesota. A lone thunderstorm initially formed over western Marshall County (Minnesota), and tracked northeast, passing west of Roseau. By that time, additional storms had formed between Jamestown and Grand Forks. This line of storms then tracked to the northeast, passing through the central and northern Red River Valley and Lake of the Woods region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado snarled and snapped trees in multiple shelterbelts along its path. Peak winds were estimated at 105 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Precipitation continued below normal across southwest Minnesota, varying from 50 to 75 percent of normal. Temperatures were well-below normal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate (D1) drought evolved to severe (D2) drought conditions early in the month. Rainfall around 50 percent of normal around Worthington contributed to the worsening short-term agricultural drought, but temperatures falling well below normal late month alleviated drought conditions. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed into northwest Iowa during the very late afternoon and early evening. As larger-scale and deeper lift forcing with the upper-level jet pushed out across the frontal boundary toward early evening, scattered thunderstorms in the environment of 30-35 knots deep-layer shear and around 1000 J/kg of MLCAPE quickly became linear with mainly damaging wind and spotty large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to quarter size fell for about 10 seconds. Crop damage is an estimate from insured losses. Information provided by the United States Department of Agriculture.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon of Sunday, October 11th, a line of thunderstorms developed along a cold front in far western Minnesota. These storms quickly became severe with wind gusts of 65 mph near Canby and Brandon, with damage around Canby. As the storms moved eastward, more stable air and less wind shear allowed for the storms to decrease in severity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few power poles were blown down near Canby.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late season cold front moved across the Northland the night of the 11th into the 12th. A broad area of showers with leading thunderstorms developed ahead of the front. While the storms generally remained well below severe limits, one stronger embedded cell did produce nickel-sized hail over part of Duluth.

An early season snow storm tracked across parts of the Northland on the 22nd. The heaviest snow was originally forecast to be centered in the Brainerd Lakes area to the I-35 corridor, but ended up setting up just to our south. This greatly cut down snowfall totals with very few places reaching the 6 inch mark.

A quick moving disturbance spread a swath of warm advection precipitation across South Dakota into southwest Minnesota during the early morning hours of October 20, 2020. While temperatures were initially warm enough to support rain, a combination of wet bulb cooling and the diabatic impact of intense lift in the mid-level frontogenetic zone caused a rapid and persistent change over to snow. A band of 3 to 7 inches of snowfall resulted from east central South Dakota near Interstate 29 eastward along the Buffalo Ridge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall, moderate at times, totaled 7.0 inches at Minneota, 6.5 inches at Marshall and 6.0 inches at Tracy and Ghent.  Visibility was frequently less than a mile between 0700 and 1100CST.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 4.5 inches at Pipestone and 3.4 inches at Edgerton, with more intense snowfall with visibility less than one mile occurring during the morning commute between 0700-0830CST.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mahnomen Airport (K3N8) AWOS reported 51 knot or 58.7 mph wind gust.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large 4 to 6 inch diameter tree branches were broken down as a thunderstorm squall moved through town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved across the Northland the afternoon of the 20th and departed during the early morning hours of the 21st bringing snowfall to parts of the region. Stronger than anticipated forcing developed across the southern CWA leading to enhanced snowfall of 4 to 7 inches in that area. Additionally, some lake enhancement was seen in the Duluth area into southern Lake County. The highest snowfall report was 7 inches near Baxter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of strong to severe thunderstorms tracked to the east-northeast through Grant County, Minnesota, during the morning of June 2nd. One storm produced quarter sized hail east of Herman.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pea to quarter sized hail fell at the White Horse Ranch east of Herman.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few thunderstorms formed along the highway 2 corridor during the early morning hours of June 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was snapped along a trail in East Grand Forks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Roughly 3 to 7 inches of rain fell along the North Branch of the Two Rivers River, causing some basement flooding and flooded roads.

For this event, the 500mb pattern featured ridging over eastern Minnesota and Wisconsin, with south-southwest flow over eastern North Dakota and the northwest quarter of Minnesota. At the surface, by the early afternoon of June 8th, a cold front had set up along a line from Cavalier to Oakes, North Dakota, with the Red River Valley into adjacent areas of the northwest quarter of Minnesota in the warm sector. In this warm sector, temperatures ranged in the 80s with dew points around 70. By the mid afternoon, thunderstorms initially formed along the boundary over northeast North Dakota and tracked into the far northwest corner of Minnesota. By late afternoon, additional storms formed around Cooperstown, North Dakota, and also tracked northeast into the far northwest corner of Minnesota. Kittson County, Minnesota, received several rounds of heavy rainfall. By mid evening, most storms tracked between Thief River Falls and Lake of the Woods. Finally, more storms formed and tracked across portions of west central Minnesota during the early morning hours of June 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The roof was peeled off a pole barn.

On June 7th, 500mb ridging had set up over eastern Minnesota into Wisconsin, with southwest flow over the Dakotas into northwest Minnesota. At the surface, by mid afternoon, a cold front had moved into central North Dakota, with a stationary boundary just to the east of the Red River Valley. It was hot and windy over eastern North Dakota into the Valley, with temperatures peaking in the upper 80s to upper 90s (Fargo set a record high of 99 degrees), and dew points in the 60s to low 70s. South winds were strong all day, gusting into the 45 mph range consistently. East of the Red River Valley, in northwest Minnesota, temperatures were cooler with lower wind speeds. By mid afternoon, thunderstorms flared up over northwest Minnesota. Through the rest of the late afternoon and evening, numerous severe storms occurred over eastern North Dakota into the Red River Valley, producing large hail, damaging winds, and even a tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The hail fell along the highway near the border.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thick morning clouds and rain held temperatures down east of the Red River Valley on June 20th. Breaks in the clouds along and west of the Valley allowed for afternoon heating and highs in the low to mid 70s. Although most severe ingredients were marginal, there was a cold pool aloft that helped sustain several pulse (briefly severe) storms. Most of these storms produced hail, although there were also a few funnel cloud reports.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains from Tropical Depression Cristobal fell across southeast Minnesota on June 9th. Rainfall totals of 1 to 3 inches were common with locally higher amounts of 4 to 5 inches. This heavy rain created some flash flooding with mudslides across portions of Fillmore and Winona Counties. Mudslides occurred near Whalan (Fillmore County) that impacted State Highway 16. Mudslides also occurred near Elba and Whitewater State Park (Winona County) and near Winona (Winona County) that brought down some power lines. The highest reported rainfall totals were 5.34 inches near Whalan and 4.79 near Winona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: South of Winona, 4.79 inches of rain fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The severe weather outbreak on Wednesday, June 17th, had a similar set up to the ones from June 7th and 8th. By the mid afternoon of June 17th, a stationary boundary was located over the central Dakotas, with eastern North Dakota and the adjacent area of the northwest quarter of Minnesota in the warm sector. Temperatures in the warm sector peaked in the upper 80s to middle 90s, with dew points in the 60s to around 70 degrees. The 500mb pattern featured ridging over the Great Lakes, with southwest flow over eastern North Dakota and northwest Minnesota. A lone thunderstorm initially formed over western Marshall County (Minnesota), and tracked northeast, passing west of Roseau. By that time, additional storms had formed between Jamestown and Grand Forks. This line of storms then tracked to the northeast, passing through the central and northern Red River Valley and Lake of the Woods region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were snapped in shelterbelts and groves.

It was hot and windy over eastern North Dakota into the Valley, with temperatures peaking in the upper 80s to upper 90s (Fargo set a record high of 99 degrees), and dew points in the 60s to low 70s. South winds were strong all day, gusting into the 45 mph range consistently. East of the Red River Valley, in northwest Minnesota, temperatures were cooler with lower wind speeds. By mid afternoon, thunderstorms flared up over northwest Minnesota. Through the rest of the late afternoon and evening, numerous severe storms occurred over eastern North Dakota into the Red River Valley, producing large hail, damaging winds, and even a tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind gust was measured by a personal weather station.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The severe weather outbreak on Wednesday, June 17th, had a similar set up to the ones from June 7th and 8th. By the mid afternoon of June 17th, a stationary boundary was located over the central Dakotas, with eastern North Dakota and the adjacent area of the northwest quarter of Minnesota in the warm sector. Temperatures in the warm sector peaked in the upper 80s to middle 90s, with dew points in the 60s to around 70 degrees. The 500mb pattern featured ridging over the Great Lakes, with southwest flow over eastern North Dakota and northwest Minnesota. A lone thunderstorm initially formed over western Marshall County (Minnesota), and tracked northeast, passing west of Roseau. By that time, additional storms had formed between Jamestown and Grand Forks. This line of storms then tracked to the northeast, passing through the central and northern Red River Valley and Lake of the Woods region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unusual warmth with a week of temperatures mainly in the 70s (even 50s and 60s on November 9 less than a day before this storm) was brought to an abrupt end as a deep trough of low pressure moved out of Rockies and into the Plains. An intense lower- to mid-level frontal zone oozed eastward as a strong jet lifted out across the Plains on November 10, initiating widespread precipitation. More rapid shallow cooling occurred across northwest Iowa and northeast Nebraska, and led to a swath of freezing rain which accumulated from a tenth to a half inch during the overnight and morning hours, before changing to snow in the afternoon. Heavier and more persistent snowfall occurred with the deeper cooling to the northwest across southeast South Dakota, far northwest Iowa and southwest Minnesota, where amounts ranged from 3 to 7 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall totaled 4.0 inches 8 miles southeast of Hendricks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For this event, the 500mb pattern featured ridging over eastern Minnesota and Wisconsin, with south-southwest flow over eastern North Dakota and the northwest quarter of Minnesota. At the surface, by the early afternoon of June 8th, a cold front had set up along a line from Cavalier to Oakes, North Dakota, with the Red River Valley into adjacent areas of the northwest quarter of Minnesota in the warm sector. In this warm sector, temperatures ranged in the 80s with dew points around 70. By the mid afternoon, thunderstorms initially formed along the boundary over northeast North Dakota and tracked into the far northwest corner of Minnesota. By late afternoon, additional storms formed around Cooperstown, North Dakota, and also tracked northeast into the far northwest corner of Minnesota. Kittson County, Minnesota, received several rounds of heavy rainfall. By mid evening, most storms tracked between Thief River Falls and Lake of the Woods. Finally, more storms formed and tracked across portions of west central Minnesota during the early morning hours of June 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and tree limbs were blown down, leading to some structural damage on the southern end of Otter Tail Lake.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moved across southeast Minnesota during the late afternoon and evening of June 28th. These storms produced two small tornadoes across Winona County. The first occurred northeast of Lewiston with very little damage reported. The second tornado touched down east of Stockton and also produced very little damage. The storms also dropped large hail ranging from nickel up to golf ball sized. The golf ball sized hail fell west of Lyle (Mower County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was sighted south of Elkton.

Temperatures in the warm sector peaked in the upper 80s to middle 90s, with dew points in the 60s to around 70 degrees. The 500mb pattern featured ridging over the Great Lakes, with southwest flow over eastern North Dakota and northwest Minnesota. A lone thunderstorm initially formed over western Marshall County (Minnesota), and tracked northeast, passing west of Roseau. By that time, additional storms had formed between Jamestown and Grand Forks. This line of storms then tracked to the northeast, passing through the central and northern Red River Valley and Lake of the Woods region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This was the third round of hail for the evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: For this event, the 500mb pattern featured ridging over eastern Minnesota and Wisconsin, with south-southwest flow over eastern North Dakota and the northwest quarter of Minnesota. At the surface, by the early afternoon of June 8th, a cold front had set up along a line from Cavalier to Oakes, North Dakota, with the Red River Valley into adjacent areas of the northwest quarter of Minnesota in the warm sector. In this warm sector, temperatures ranged in the 80s with dew points around 70. By the mid afternoon, thunderstorms initially formed along the boundary over northeast North Dakota and tracked into the far northwest corner of Minnesota. By late afternoon, additional storms formed around Cooperstown, North Dakota, and also tracked northeast into the far northwest corner of Minnesota. Kittson County, Minnesota, received several rounds of heavy rainfall. By mid evening, most storms tracked between Thief River Falls and Lake of the Woods. Finally, more storms formed and tracked across portions of west central Minnesota during the early morning hours of June 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On June 7th, 500mb ridging had set up over eastern Minnesota into Wisconsin, with southwest flow over the Dakotas into northwest Minnesota. At the surface, by mid afternoon, a cold front had moved into central North Dakota, with a stationary boundary just to the east of the Red River Valley. It was hot and windy over eastern North Dakota into the Valley, with temperatures peaking in the upper 80s to upper 90s (Fargo set a record high of 99 degrees), and dew points in the 60s to low 70s. South winds were strong all day, gusting into the 45 mph range consistently. East of the Red River Valley, in northwest Minnesota, temperatures were cooler with lower wind speeds. By mid afternoon, thunderstorms flared up over northwest Minnesota. Through the rest of the late afternoon and evening, numerous severe storms occurred over eastern North Dakota into the Red River Valley, producing large hail, damaging winds, and even a tornado.

Heavy rains from Tropical Depression Cristobal fell across southeast Minnesota on June 9th. Rainfall totals of 1 to 3 inches were common with locally higher amounts of 4 to 5 inches. This heavy rain created some flash flooding with mudslides across portions of Fillmore and Winona Counties. Mudslides occurred near Whalan (Fillmore County) that impacted State Highway 16. Mudslides also occurred near Elba and Whitewater State Park (Winona County) and near Winona (Winona County) that brought down some power lines. The highest reported rainfall totals were 5.34 inches near Whalan and 4.79 near Winona.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains from Tropical Depression Cristobal caused mudslides to occur near Whalan. One slide impacted State Highway 16.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains from Tropical Depression Cristobal caused a mudslide to occur near Elba and Whitewater State Park. Other mudslides near Winona brought down power poles. High water covered roads near Lewiston.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms moved across southeast Minnesota during the late afternoon and evening of June 28th. These storms produced two small tornadoes across Winona County. The first occurred northeast of Lewiston with very little damage reported. The second tornado touched down east of Stockton and also produced very little damage. The storms also dropped large hail ranging from nickel up to golf ball sized. The golf ball sized hail fell west of Lyle (Mower County).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A short-lived tornado was on the ground for about two minutes northeast of Stockton. The tornado only produced some minor tree damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated thunderstorm formed over north central North Dakota during the afternoon of June 11th and tracked south-southeast. It eventually crossed into northwest Minnesota north of Oslo, Minnesota, where it continued to drop large hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: By the early evening of Thursday, August 13th, an area of surface low pressure had set up near Mayville, North Dakota. A warm front extended out to the east-southeast from the low. South of the warm front, temperatures were in the mid to upper 80s, with dew points in the lower 70s. Thunderstorms broke out along and north of the warm front, producing large hail, damaging wind gusts, and two tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large hail fell in east central Smiley Township.

During the very early morning hours of Friday, August 14th, sporadic slow moving severe thunderstorms formed across portions of west central Minnesota. These resulted in reports of large hail, strong wind gusts, and flash flooding. Prior to sunrise and through Friday morning, additional storms developed back westward into southeast North Dakota, and they continued to fire across west central Minnesota. These storms continued to produce large hail, gusty winds, and flash flooding. Conditions with temperatures at record or near-record warm levels on December 22 underwent a drastic change over a brief 24 hour period, as a sharp upper-level trough in westerly flow amplified while translating from the Pacific Northwest to the Plains.  Low pressure developed and deepened rapidly while moving from South Dakota around 0000CST December 23 to the northern Great Lakes by 0600CST December 24.||Very steep lower to mid-level lapse rates and lift focused within a 200-250hPa deep layer supportive of effective dendritic growth resulted in intense precipitation banding over a 5 to 8 hour period. Winds in the lower mixed layer reached 40 to 60 knots as Arctic air surged into the area behind the system, producing numerous hours of widespread whiteout conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds gusted as high as 60 mph near Rushmore and 55 mph at Worthington, and despite a lack of pre-storm snow cover, snowfall which accumulated to 4.0 inches at Worthington and 3.3 inches 4 miles south of Worthington resulted in widespread whiteout conditions for several hours.  As vehicles slowed for very poor visibility, several were rear-ended by faster moving vehicles which resulted in a multi-vehicle accident involving at least a dozen drivers which eventually blocked a bridge along Interstate 90 east of Worthington.  The very poor visibility also resulted in numerous stranded motorists, which were unable to be seen by rescue vehicles.  County shelters were opened in Ellsworth, Wilmont and Adrian. Minnesota Department of Transportation shut down all main roads during the blizzard across southwest MN due to poor visibility and numerous accidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On Wednesday, December 23rd, blizzard conditions were observed across much of Minnesota as an intense weather system moved from west to east through the region. It produced several inches of snow with rates as high as 1-2 inches per hour at times, with a bit of rain falling ahead of the snow which led to ice-covered roadways. Temperatures plummeted with strong winds gusting as high as 50-65 mph out of the northwest behind the low-pressure system. Temperatures reached as high as the mid-40s during the morning before bottoming out in the single digits above zero to near zero by midnight. ||A robust mid to upper-level trough approaching the region continued to strengthen before closing off, with a surface response in the form of a deepening low-pressure system. This combined with incredible mid-level lapse rates to produce significant lift and widespread precipitation, with an intense pressure gradient resulting in strong winds as the system moved eastwards. ||Winds intensified alongside bands of heavy snow during the afternoon, which resulted in widespread blizzard conditions as visibilities dropped below 1/4 mile across the region and remained low for several hours. Snowfall diminished during the evening and into early Thursday morning but winds remained strong enough to support blizzard conditions into mid-morning Thursday in some spots. ||Some of the higher wind speeds and snowfall totals during this event included:||Redwood Falls AP, 68 MPH                        |Crystal AP, 66 MPH                       |Madison AP, 63 MPH|Montevideo AP, 62 MPH                        |5 miles south of Marietta, 62 MPH          |Fairmont AP, 61 MPH                      |2 miles west of Clara City, 61 MPH                        |Appleton AP, 60 MPH                                      |Winsted, 58 MPH                        |Benson AP, 58 MPH                        |Granite Falls AP, 58 MPH                        |Minneapolis-St. Paul AP, 58 MPH       ||2 miles west of Coon Rapids, 9.5 inches|4 miles southeast of East Bethel, 9.5 inches  |2 miles west of Excelsior, 9.0 inches             |2 miles southwest of Maple Grove, 9.0 inches|Cannon Falls, 9.0 inches   |MSP International Airport,  8.8 inches     |1 mile west of Carver, 8.4 inches             |NWS Chanhassen, 8.2 inches    ||Impacts included significant slowdowns to air travel at airports across the region including MSP, ice and snow-covered roads that led to no travel orders and closed highways, and even National Guard rescues at the Governor's request. This was exacerbated due to the timing of the system falling on December 23rd, which is one of the busiest overall travel days of the year before the holidays.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mainly rain fell Wednesday morning and early afternoon across the county. As much colder air arrived during the afternoon, temperatures dropped quickly which changed a mixture of precipitation over to snow. The snow became heavy by the late afternoon. By early evening, blizzard conditions developed and continued through the evening. Wind gusts of 25 to 40 mph were frequently achieved during the height of the storm.

The surface low pressure area and cold front tracked from west to east across the region from the morning into the afternoon hours. Snowfall amounts of 2 to 4 inches occurred. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure brought heavy snowfall and very strong winds to much of the Northland starting on Wednesday the 23rd and lingering into Thursday the 24th. This storm was unique in that widespread blizzard conditions occurred across the upper Midwest, including areas from the Brainerd Lakes east towards the North Shore, Twin Ports, and Interstate 35 corridor with many observed wind gusts between 50 and 70 mph. Very strong frontogenesis occurred along a trowal axis that extended across much of northern Wisconsin west into Minnesota. Warm air advection caused temperatures across most of east-central Minnesota to rise above freezing and produce mixed precipitation for several hours during the day. As the cold front gradually moved east, temperatures very quickly dropped and caused treacherous road conditions as mixed precipitation changed over to snow. Narrow bands of heavy snow set up over the Brainerd Lakes northeast towards the Iron Range during the early morning and early afternoon, and then translated east towards the Twin Ports, North Shore, and Interstate 35 corridor. The heavy snow bands coincided with the strong mid-level frontogenesis, which also produced very strong northwest winds that caused the widespread blizzard conditions. A maximum gust of 70 mph was recorded at the Bay of Grand Marais during the height of the storm. Damage was reported to buildings and other infrastructure, in addition to some power outages and widespread whiteout conditions that made travel difficult if not impossible.||As the cold front swept rapidly eastward, bitterly cold air moved into the region and led to the development of a topographically-forced gravity wave during the night of the 23rd that dropped several more inches of snow from Two Harbors to Superior to Holyoke. The eastern half of northeast Minnesota received 6 to 10 inches of snowfall with lesser amounts further west in addition to the widespread blizzard conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The season's first winter storm struck the area through the late morning and afternoon of Wednesday, December 23, 2020. This area was just to the northeast of the track of a strong blizzard and encompassed portions of northwest Minnesota which encountered light to moderate snow, strong winds, and dangerous wind chills which in combination also created periods of life threatening conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A winter storm produced from 1 to 4 inches of snow, winds gusting to 45 mph at times, and wind chills of -25F or colder.
